Achieving Consistent and Comparable Climate-related Disclosures: 2024 Progress
resources_with_topic.notes:
The Financial Stability Board (FSB) published [url=https://www.fsb.org/uploads/P121124.pdf][i]Achieving Consistent and Comparable Climate-related Disclosures: 2024 Progress[/i][/url], which describes the progress FSB jurisdictions, standard-setters, and international organizations have made in achieving consistent and comparable climate-related disclosures. The FSB, which includes the U.S. Federal Reserve, Treasury, and Securities and Exchange Commission, along with representatives from G20 economies, was formed in 2009 to coordinate national financial authorities and international standard-setting bodies in developing strong regulatory, supervisory, and other financial sector policies.

The report finds that around 75% of FSB jurisdictions have implemented or proposed voluntary or mandatory climate-related disclosure requirements based on the Task Force on Climate-related Financial Disclosures (TCFD) recommendations; NAIC climate disclosure standards follow TCFD guidelines. The International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB), the standard-setting body established by the International Financial Reporting Standards (IFRS) Foundation, established the S1 and S2 sustainability disclosure standards in 2023; S2 formalizes TCFD principles. The report finds that approximately 57% of global GDP is represented by jurisdictions that have progressed toward adopting or aligning with the ISSB’s IFRS S1 and S2 standards. The report finds that adoption faces challenges, particularly in emerging markets and developing economies (EMDEs), due to regulatory uncertainty, limited data availability, perceived costs, and capacity gaps.

The FSB emphasizes the critical integration of sustainability-related disclosures with financial and prudential reporting, with initiatives such as the Basel Committee’s Pillar 3 framework helping to harmonize reporting requirements. Enhancing the reliability of disclosures is a key focus, with efforts to establish robust global assurance frameworks. This aligns with the IAASB ISSA 5000, which supports consistent assurance practices across jurisdictions.

resources_with_topic.notes: [b]The European Supervisory Authorities (EBA, EIOPA, and ESMA – the ESAs), together with the European Central Bank (ECB), [/b][color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://www.eiopa.europa.eu/document/download/6a90e0a7-135e-41f0-af69-22065a0bf8cd_en?filename=Report%20on%20fit%20for%2055%20climate%20scenario%20analysis.pdf][b][i]"Fit-For-55” climate scenario analysis[/i][/b][/url][/color][b][i], [/i]finds that climate transition risks (i.e., adapting to and mitigating climate-related risks) alone are unlikely to threaten financial stability. [/b]The European Union’s [i]Fit-for-55[/i] package aims to stimulate investment and innovation in the transition to a green economy, playing a key role in the E.U.’s goal to achieve an emissions reduction of 55% by 2030 and climate neutrality by 2050. It aims to align E.U. legislation with these goals through policies that include—among others—the E.U. emissions trading system, the carbon border adjustment mechanism, sector-specific emissions targets, and revisions to the renewable energy and energy efficiency directive. The European Commission [color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://www.eba.europa.eu/sites/default/files/document_library/About%20Us/Missions%20and%20tasks/Correspondence%20with%20EU%20institutions/2023/Assessment%20of%20the%20financial%20system%E2%80%99s%20resilience%20to%20stress%20in%20the%20transition%20to%20the%20EU%E2%80%99s%202030%20goals%20for%20the%20reduction%20of%20greenhouse%20gas%20emissions/Man/1052659/Mandate%20for%20the%20FF55%20one-off%20exercise.pdf]invited[/url][/color] the ESAs and the ECB to assess the impact on the insurance and other financial sectors and the potential for contagion and amplification effects across the financial system. The study did find that the interaction of adverse macro-financial developments with transition risk factors could disrupt the evolving transition and substantially increase financial institutions’ losses, thereby impairing their financing capacity.

resources_with_topic.Name: SAPWG Adoptions (updated 11/17/2024)
Instructions and Guidance on the Application of the Prudent Person Principle
resources_with_topic.notes:
In response to structural shifts in insurers' business models, including increased allocation to complex, illiquid, and hard-to-value assets, the Bermuda Monetary Authority (BMA) has increased its supervisory oversight. In this latest round, the BMA proposes [color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://cdn.bma.bm/documents/2024-12-04-13-09-32-Consultation-Paper---Proposed-Instructions-and-Guidanceon-the-Application-of-the-Prudent-Person-Principle.pdf]Instructions and Guidance on the Application of the Prudent Person Principle[/url][/color] (PPP), building on the existing PPP implementation requirements. The PPP requires that an insurer only assume investment risks that it can properly identify, measure, respond to, monitor, control, and report on while considering its capital requirements and adequacy, short-term and long-term liquidity requirements, and policyholder obligations. Key points of the proposal include:
[ml][ul][li indent=0 align=left][b]Investment strategy.[/b] Insurers must adopt investment strategies prioritizing capital preservation and returns while aligning with policyholder obligations and liquidity needs.[/li][li indent=0 align=left][b]Investment risk management.[/b] A robust framework for managing investment risks, including stress testing and monitoring concentrations, liquidity mismatches, and derivatives usage.[/li][li indent=0 align=left][b]Governance.[/b] Boards and senior management are responsible for overseeing compliance with PPP, with a particular focus on governance structures, conflicts of interest, and risk monitoring.[/li][li indent=0 align=left][b]Outsourcing of investment-related services.[/b] When investment management is outsourced, insurers must ensure adherence to the PPP by external asset managers and retain adequate in-house expertise.[/li][li indent=0 align=left][b]Asset-Liability Matching.[/b] Ensuring investments align with liabilities’ nature, liquidity, and duration.[/li][li indent=0 align=left][b]Risk concentration, risk accumulation, and diversification. [/b]An insurer’s investment policy and limits should be such that individual investments and exposures to different asset classes and risk factors are held and maintained at prudent levels.[/li][li indent=0 align=left][b]Complex and non-publicly traded assets[/b]. In considering investing in complex investments, insurers should comprehensively assess the inherent risks that may be present, including the heightened risk of large, sudden, or unexpected losses due to limited transparency and information flow, volatility misestimation, and difficulties in identifying and reflecting changes in risk profile before significant deterioration.[/li][li indent=0 align=left][b]Solvency self-assessment and group solvency self-assessment. [/b]Insurers’ own risk and solvency self-assessment should be evidence and attest to their application of PPP and the considered impact on their solvency and liquidity positions.[/li][li indent=0 align=left][b]Use of derivatives and other financial instruments. [/b]An insurer choosing to engage in derivative activities should clearly define its objectives, investment and derivative strategy, governance mechanism, and derivative use policies and processes. Derivatives should be considered in the context of a prudent overall asset-liability management strategy.[/li][/ul][/ml]

resources_with_topic.Name: Insurance Group Supervision Framework
resources_with_topic.notes:
The Bermuda Monetary Authority (BMA) is proposing significant enhancements to its [color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=http://file///C:/Users/amnon/Downloads/2024-12-04-16-17-14-Consultation-Paper---Proposed-Enhancements-to-the-Insurance-Group-Supervision-Framework-Package-Current.pdf][i]Insurance Group Supervision Framework[/i][/url][/color] to align more closely with global standards, including the International Association of Insurance Supervisors (IAIS) Common Framework (ComFrame) and Insurance Core Principles (ICPs). Key aspects of the consultation paper (i.e., request for comment) include:
[ml][ul][li indent=0 align=left][b]Mandatory Group Supervision. [/b]The BMA will enforce mandatory group supervision if the ultimate parent entity of an insurance group is incorporated in Bermuda and oversees insurance operations both locally and abroad. This moves towards a direct supervision model, focusing on the parent entity.[/li][li indent=0 align=left][b]Definition and Supervision of Insurance Holding Companies. [/b]The proposal defines an [i]insurance holding company[/i] as any Bermuda-incorporated entity holding stakes in insurers. The BMA will directly regulate these entities when deemed critical to the group's financial health.[/li][li indent=0 align=left][b]Transition from Designated Insurers. [/b]The current framework uses a hybrid model, where group supervision often operates through a [i]designated insurer.[/i] The proposal suggests phasing out this approach, giving the BMA direct powers over parent or holding companies​.[/li][li indent=0 align=left][b]Direct Regulatory Powers. [/b]The proposal includes a robust set of powers for direct oversight, ensuring compliance, and addressing risks associated with insurance groups and their holding companies​.[/li][/ul][/ml]

resources_with_topic.Name: Global Insurance Market Report (GIMAR)
resources_with_topic.notes:
The International Association of Insurance Supervisors (IAIS) December 3, 2024, [color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://www.iaisweb.org/uploads/2024/12/Global-Insurance-Market-Report-2024.pdf?utm_source=International+Association+of+Insurance+Supervisors+%28IAIS%29&utm_campaign=de2263c0a0-EMAIL_CAMPAIGN_2024_12_02_11_55&utm_medium=email&utm_term=0_-de2263c0a0-634247980]Global Insurance Market Report (GMAR)[/url][/color] summarizes results from its Global Monitoring Exercise (GME), which monitors trends and systemic risks in the insurance sector using data to the end of 2023. The IAIS is the principal international organization of insurance supervisors, creating international standards and guidelines on insurance supervision. The IAIS influences national and regional regulators by publishing supervisory and supporting material. The GME evaluates global insurance market trends and developments and identifies potential systemic risks. The GME includes the Individual Insurer Monitoring (IIM) assessment, which assesses 59 of the largest insurers covering over 90% of global written premiums, aggregate trends, and ancillary indicators to supplement the primary individual assessments. 

The report includes a rich analysis of global trends. We focus only on Section 3, which outlines two themes identified through the 2024 GME that remain a priority for supervisors: (1) macroeconomic risks and (2) structural shifts in the life insurance sector related to the use of alternative investments and cross-border asset-intensive reinsurance, which the IAIS has been assessing since 2020. It includes tabulations of key concerns regulators raise, which can provide foresight into regulatory trends.

[b]Macroeconomic risks, [/b]Section 3.1, analyses global and regional (e.g., North America, which covers the U.S., Canada, Bermuda, etc.) trends that include liquidity and credit risk. While these macroeconomic risks have been reasonably stable, pockets of risk, notably commercial real estate, were identified as growing.  

[b]Structural shifts in the life insurance sector,[/b] Section 3.2, analyzes (1) increased investments in [i]alternative assets[/i] such as private credit, private equity, and securitizations and (2) the increased use of cross-border asset-intensive reinsurance.

[b]Increased investments in [i]alternative assets[/i][/b][i]. [/i]To its credit, the IAIS acknowledges the absence of a definition for [i]alternative assets [/i]and is working to develop one based on three key principles: valuation uncertainty, illiquidity, and complexity. GME data provides a means for the IAIS to quantify potential concerns around alternative assets, such as valuation uncertainty, illiquidity, assets originated by related parties, and assets held under asset-intensive reinsurance agreements. Figure 26 from the report (reproduced) ranks discretionary valuation, liquidity risks, and hidden leverage as primary risks associated with alternative assets.
 
[center][img width=500]https://mcusercontent.com/79c3cb5ae113df9ea96a5e443/images/53d91311-720a-6e15-3bf5-eb7567fddead.png[/img][/center]

[b]Increased use of cross-border asset-intensive reinsurance. [/b]The report outlines the benefits of reinsurance, including sharing risks across blocks of business, providing new sources of capital, and diversifying investment risk by transferring risk to reinsurers with insurers that can access a wider range of investment opportunities. The report also outlines the primary concerns:
[ml][ul][li indent=0 align=left]Regulatory arbitrage across jurisdictions might be motivating transactions.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]Given the limited number of reinsurers and jurisdictions that dominate the transaction volumes, concentration risks are generated.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]The lack of understanding of (changing) prudential frameworks in different jurisdictions may hinder effective supervision.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]Limitations with information result in a limited understanding of the spectrum of risks.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]Potential conflicts of interest exist in corporate structures, where the asset manager’s profitability goals may influence the reinsurer’s risk appetite.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]The increasing complexity of these agreements, including retrocession and special purpose vehicles, limits transparency and understanding of the associated risks.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]Concerning reserving, questions arise about the methods and frequency with which insurers and supervisors must review the adequacy of compliance with the accounting standards.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]While recapture risk in certain forms of asset-intensive reinsurance is limited, handling recaptures may present challenges for insurers and supervisors due to a lack of precedent and potential complexities of illiquid or complex assets.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]Differentiating between assets supporting ceded and retained liabilities in financial statements can be challenging.[/li][/ul][/ml]Table 1 (reproduced) reports key supervisory concerns with asset-intensive reinsurance, including potential concentration risks, increasing complexity, and potential conflicts of interest.
 
[center][img width=500]https://mcusercontent.com/79c3cb5ae113df9ea96a5e443/images/72d06a52-6bf9-c082-a24d-b7fd30de1a84.png[/img][/center]
[b]What's next?[/b] 
[ml][ul][li indent=0 align=left]With the IAIS's role as the principal international organization of insurance supervisors, expect top-ranked concerns to be a focus of regulatory reforms.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]The IAIS is drafting an Issues Paper on these structural shifts in the life insurance sector. The paper will focus on the increased allocation to [i]alternative assets[/i] (including a definition of [i]alternative assets[/i]) and asset-intensive reinsurance agreements. It will be published for consultation (i.e., posted with a request for comment) in March 2025.[/li][/ul][/ml]

resources_with_topic.Name: BoE Financial Systemwide Stress Exercise
resources_with_topic.notes:
The Bank of England (BoE) November 29, 2024, report, [color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://www.bankofengland.co.uk/financial-stability/boe-system-wide-exploratory-scenario-exercise/boe-swes-exercise-final-report][i]Systemwide Exploratory Scenario Exercise[/i][/url][/color][i] [/i](SWES), explores how the U.K. financial system would respond to a market shock, aiming to:
[ml][ol][li indent=0 align=left]Assess risks to and from non-bank financial institutions (NBFIs), which includes insurers, in stress and[/li][li indent=0 align=left]Investigate how market dynamics can amplify market shocks and potentially pose risks to U.K. financial stability.[/li][/ol][/ml][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]The SWES is a [/color][i][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]unique systemwide[/color][/i][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)] exercise in that it captures complex behaviors under stress; systemwide analyses carried out by central banks have tended to be model-based without the direct participation of firms. Around 50 participating firms, including banks, insurers, pension schemes, hedge funds, asset managers, and central counterparties, were asked to evaluate how they would be affected by and respond to a hypothetical stress scenario. The exercise is not a test of the resilience of the individual participants. Its focus is systemwide, including on important U.K. financial markets and their resilience in stress. As with any exercise, the scenario, actions, and outcomes are hypothetical, not a forecast.[/color]

[b][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]The impact on insurers.[/color][/b][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)] The report breaks down the impact on major segments, including insurers (see Annex 4). The hypothetical shock causes insurers to face significant liquidity needs comparable in magnitude to those they faced during the 2022 gilt market dysfunction. The liquidity needs stem mainly from variation margin (VM) calls on derivatives positions, which are sensitive to increases in interest rates and sterling depreciation. Insurers primarily meet these margin calls by pledging assets from their large stocks of eligible securities, including corporate bond collateral. The report explains that insurers' ability to do so reflects steps they have taken since 2022, including the right to post corporate bonds as collateral under the terms of the credit support annexes (CSAs) of their agreements with banks – so-called [/color][i][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]dirty[/color][/i][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)] CSAs. [/color]

[color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]The exercise also included a shock to occur when internal risk metrics approached [/color][i][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]amber[/color][/i][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]. Asset sales were typically seen as a last resort and would depend on the market environment, with firms typically preferring to draw on committed repo facilities and existing revolving credit facilities in the first instance, with some firms also seeking to use repo to upgrade their collateral pool and others settling derivatives transactions. Insurers also reported that they would not be materially impacted by a temporary freezing of the repo market during a period of extreme market stress.[/color]

[b][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]Conclusions from the exercise and next steps include:[/color][/b]
[ml][ul][li indent=0 align=left]The exercise highlighted the resilience of the core U.K. financial system.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]Repo market resilience is central to supporting core markets in stress, and further policy work to increase it would be beneficial and complement the role of existing central bank facilities.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]Authorities and market participants have taken action following recent market shocks, which has improved gilt market resilience, but further work is required.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]The sterling corporate bond market could face a [i]jump to illiquidity[/i] in stress and, if disruption were persistent, financing for the real economy. Authorities are planning to take action to understand and reduce risks in sterling corporate bond markets.[/li][/ul][/ml]

resources_with_topic.Name: December 5, 2024 SSG Presentation - Update on Modeling Intrinsic Price Designations for CLOs
LATF Meeting Agenda - December 12, 2024
Liquidity Preparedness for Margin and Collateral Calls
resources_with_topic.notes:
The Financial Stability Board (FBC) published its final report, [url=https://www.fsb.org/uploads/P101224-1.pdf][i]Liquidity Preparedness for Margin and Collateral Calls[/i][/url]; the FSB had requested comment on its previous April 17, 2024, report. The report highlights the need for policy adjustments to deal with liquidity strains with non-bank financial intermediation (NBFI) arising from spikes in margin and collateral calls during market stress, such as the March 2020 market turmoil. NBFI includes life insurers who rely heavily on long-dated interest rate derivatives, which can be susceptible to margin calls. The FSB proposes eight high-level and cross-sectoral policy recommendations, noting that the regulatory landscape for NBFIs varies significantly. For example, while Solvency II in the E.U. and the U.K. has general requirements for insurance firms to assess and manage liquidity risk, there are no specific margin and collateral call rules. The FSB, which includes the U.S. Federal Reserve, Treasury, Securities and Exchange Commission, and representatives from G20 economies, was formed in 2009 to coordinate national financial authorities and international standard-setting bodies to develop strong regulatory, supervisory, and other financial sector policies. The FSB has been flagging concerns over liquidity risks, and in October 2024, the FSB published [url=https://www.fsb.org/uploads/P231024.pdf][i]Depositor Behaviour and Interest Rate and Liquidity Risks in the Financial System Lessons from the March 2023 Banking Turmoil[/i][/url], which points to margin and collateral calls on derivative positions or securities financing transactions as a main channel whereby insurers are most vulnerable to solvency and liquidity risks. 

The report provides eight policy recommendations intended to build on existing rules for governing non-bank market participants, including insurers, in managing margin and collateral calls in centrally and non-centrally cleared derivatives and securities markets, including securities financing such as repo (paraphrased):
[ml][ol][li indent=0 align=left]Liquidity risk arising from exposure to margin and collateral call spikes should be included in liquidity risk management and governance frameworks.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]Establish liquidity risk tolerances for margin and collateral calls and contingency funding plans to meet liquidity needs.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]Liquidity risk frameworks need to be reviewed regularly to ensure their ongoing effectiveness in mitigating liquidity risk exposures to spikes in margin and collateral calls, including during times of stress.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]Conduct liquidity stress tests to identify strain sources and ensure adequate funding and collateral.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]There is a need for liquidity stress tests to cover a range of extreme but plausible scenarios, including both backward-looking and hypothetical scenarios.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]There is a need for resilient and effective operational processes and collateral management practices.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]There is a need for sufficient cash, readily available and diverse liquid assets, and collateral arrangements to meet margin and collateral calls.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]There is a need for active, transparent, and regular interactions with counterparties and third-party service providers in collateralized transactions.[/li][/ol][/ml][b]Why does this matter? [/b]While the FSB is not a regulator, it shapes international policy by promoting financial stability and encouraging coherent policies across jurisdictions. For life insurers, the recommendations are particularly relevant given their potential exposure to liquidity risks (e.g., coming from surrenders or margin calls) in stressed markets. Regulatory bodies like the International Association of Insurance Supervisors (IAIS) will likely consider these insights, signaling heightened expectations for liquidity risk management and disclosure in the insurance sector. Insurers should anticipate evolving regulations and adjust their strategies accordingly.

resources_with_topic.Name: Closing Liquidity Reporting Gaps and Streamlining Standard Formula Reporting (CP19/24)
resources_with_topic.notes:
The Bank of England (BoE) Prudential Regulation Authority (PRA) is requesting comments on [color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://www.bankofengland.co.uk/prudential-regulation/publication/2024/december/closing-liquidity-reporting-gaps-consultation-paper]Closing Liquidity Reporting Gaps and Streamlining Standard Formula Reporting[/url][/color] (CP19/24), which would heighten disclosure for large insurance firms with significant exposure to derivatives or securities involved in lending or repurchase agreements. The proposal sits alongside broader efforts to monitor macroprudential vulnerabilities resulting from liquidity risks at insurers and other types of non-bank financial institutions (NBFI), including the recent [color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://www.bankofengland.co.uk/financial-stability/boe-system-wide-exploratory-scenario-exercise]system-wide exploratory scenario (SWES)[/url][/color] and the Financial Stability Board’s work on [color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://www.fsb.org/2024/04/liquidity-preparedness-for-margin-and-collateral-calls-consultation-report/]NBFI preparedness for Margin and Collateral Calls[/url][/color] (see [color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://mailchi.mp/bridgewayanalytics/newsreel-17315081?e=9ea3ec665c]ART Newsreel | December 5, 2024[/url][/color]). 

The PRA describes its expectations for effective management of liquidity risk in [color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://www.bankofengland.co.uk/prudential-regulation/publication/2019/liquidity-risk-management-for-insurers-ss]Supervisory Statement (SS) 5/19 – liquidity risk management for insurers[/url][/color], which the proposal builds on. The proposal explains that market stresses over the last five years have revealed shortcomings in life insurers’ liquidity risk management frameworks and exposed critical gaps in liquidity reporting to the PRA, notably:
[ml][ul][li indent=0 align=left][b]‘Dash for cash’[/b]—during the uncertain early stages of the COVID-19 pandemic in March 2020, market participants sought to protect their finances by selling riskier investments and even safer assets like government bonds. This sudden ‘dash for cash’ meant there was significant volatility in market pricing, leading to spikes in margin and collateral calls under derivatives contracts held by U.K. insurers – significantly increasing liquidity risk.[/li][li indent=0 align=left][b]LDI episode[/b]—In September 2022, a U.K. fiscal policy announcement triggered a sudden fall in gilt prices, amplified by forced selling from LDI funds. The resulting movements in gilts and sterling meant U.K. insurers needed to raise a material amount of liquidity to meet margin calls on derivative positions initiated as [color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://www.bankofengland.co.uk/-/media/boe/files/letter/2022/october/letter-from-jon-cunliffe-ldi-18-october-2022.pdf]hedges for interest rate and foreign exchange (FX) risks[/url][/color].[/li][/ul][/ml]The proposal includes the introduction of four new reporting templates:
[ml][ul][li indent=0 align=left][b]Cash Flow Mismatch Template[/b]. Captures gaps in expected inflows and outflows.[/li][li indent=0 align=left][b]Cash Flow Mismatch (Short Form).[/b] Simplified version for smaller firms.[/li][li indent=0 align=left][b]Committed Facilities Template.[/b] Details available funding sources.[/li][li indent=0 align=left][b]Liquidity Market Risk Sensitivities Template.[/b] Measures sensitivity to market conditions.[/li][/ul][/ml][b]What's next? [/b]A response is requested by March 31, 2025, and the proposed implementation date is December 31, 2025.

U.S. Insurers’ Collateralized Loan Obligation (CLO) Exposure Continues to Climb in 2023 but at a Slower Pace
resources_with_topic.notes:
The NAIC’s Capital Markets Bureau published its latest report, [color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://content.naic.org/sites/default/files/capital-markets-special-reports-clo-ye2023-final.pdf][i]U.S. Insurers’ Collateralized Loan Obligation (CLO) Exposure Continues to Climb in 2023 but at a Slower Pace[/i][/url][/color]. The report summarizes familiar 2023 CLO investment trends, which we reported details on in our [i]In the Weeds[/i] columns in [color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://mailchi.mp/bridgewayanalytics/newsreel-16531344?e=9ea3ec665c]ART Newsreel | May 2, 2024[/url][/color], for life companies, and [color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://mailchi.mp/bridgewayanalytics/newsreel-16532080?e=9ea3ec665c]ART Newsreel | May 9, 2024[/url][/color], for property & casualty. A few highlights from the report: 
[ml][ul][li indent=0 align=left]U.S. insurers’ CLO holdings were $271.2 (3.2% of total cash and invested assets) at year-end 2023, a 9.5% increase from $247.7 billion at year-end 2022. Life holdings increased, reaching $217.1 billion in 2023 from $192.3 billion in 2022, while property & casualty decreased to $46.3 billion in 2023 from $47.4 in 2022.  [/li][li indent=0 align=left]The pace of growth in insurers’ CLO investments was slower in 2023, down from almost 15% in 2022 and the 20% range between 2019 and 2021.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]The credit quality of U.S. insurers’ CLO investments remained predominantly investment grade, with tranches rated BBB and higher accounting for 82% of total CLO investments and those rated AAA increasing one percentage point to 41%.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]U.S. insurers’ exposure to commercial real estate (CRE) CLOs has also increased over the last few years (Chart 5 reproduced). The Capital Markets Bureau, [color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://content.naic.org/sites/default/files/capital-markets-primer-commercial-real-estate-loan-obligations.pdf][i]CRE CLO Primer[/i][/url][/color] provides useful context to this asset class and how it differs from traditional Commercial Mortgage Backed Securities. The typical investment includes short-term floating CRE loans, usually three to five years, departing from traditional fixed-rate and long-term investments under a CMBS structure. It has been argued that shorter-duration investments allow the CLO structure to manage its longer-duration liabilities better through market fluctuations. U.S. insurers held approximately $17 billion in CRE CLOs at year-end 2023, representing a 27% increase from $13.4 billion at year-end 2022.[/li][/ul][/ml][center][img width=500]https://mcusercontent.com/79c3cb5ae113df9ea96a5e443/images/19f909ca-1e9d-c15d-52db-273218ce5464.png[/img][/center]

resources_with_topic.Name: Repack & Derivative Investments SAPWG Proposal (Attachment C SSAP#2024-16)
December 2024 Financial Stability Report
resources_with_topic.notes:
[color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]The European Insurance and Occupational Pension Authority (EIOPA) published its [/color][color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://www.eiopa.europa.eu/document/download/024725cf-a9e7-4727-9f14-2a4fed6c6d66_en?filename=EIOPA%20Financial%20Stability%20Report%20December%202024.pdf]December 2024 Financial Stability Report[/url][/color][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)], which includes an analysis of offshore asset-intensive reinsurance. The report points to the increasing use of cross-border asset-intensive reinsurance (or funded reinsurance), which is facing increased regulatory scrutiny in several jurisdictions. It points to the June 2023 U.K. [/color][color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://www.bankofengland.co.uk/-/media/boe/files/prudential-regulation/letter/2023/june/thematic-review-work-on-funded-reinsurance-arrangements.pdf]Prudential Regulation Authority (PRA) warnings over the systemic use of funded reinsurance[/url][/color][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)] and the [/color][color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://www.iaisweb.org/2023/07/iais-publishes-preview-of-2023-global-monitoring-exercise-results/]International Association of Insurance Supervisors (IAIS) examination of supervisory cross-border asset-intensive reinsurance[/url][/color][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)].[/color]
[color=rgb(34, 34, 34)] [/color]
[b][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]EIOPA finds that of Europe's EUR 6,340 billion 2023 total life liabilities, only about 2.5% is reinsured, and less than 1% is reinsured offshore.[/color][/b][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)] The report concludes that reinsurance cannot be considered a source of systemic risk for the European life insurance sector.[/color]

[b][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]What's next?[/color][/b][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)] EIOPA plans to continue monitoring developments regarding the use of offshore asset-intensive reinsurance and will work toward improved Solvency II reporting on reinsurance.[/color]

resources_with_topic.Name: Evolution of Asset Intensive Insurance Report
resources_with_topic.notes:
[color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://cdn.bma.bm/documents/2024-12-13-16-43-44-Evolution-of-Asset-Intensive-Insurance-Report-White-Paper.pdf][i]Evolution of Asset Intensive Insurance Report[/i][/url][/color], is an update to the BMA December 18, 2023, report [color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://cdn.bma.bm/documents/2024-02-12-11-00-50-Supervision-and-Regulation-of-Private-Equity-Insurers.pdf][i]Supervision and Regulation of Private Equity (PE) Insurers in Bermuda[/i][/url][/color][i]. [/i]The paper aims to enhance the understanding of the potential risks of asset-intensive insurance businesses, including strategic alliances between life and annuity insurers and asset management firms and their governance. It argues that strategic alliances between life and annuity insurers and asset management firms have played a significant role in narrowing the estimated $51 trillion global protection gap, with private capital and tools such as reinsurance playing a critical role. However, these partnerships have led to a notable shift in traditional investment strategies and pose unique risks and supervisory challenges, primarily arising from two main risk drivers:
[ml][ul][li indent=0 align=left]Potential governance, conflict, and risk management challenges; and[/li][li indent=0 align=left]A tendency to invest in higher proportions of illiquid assets, which are subject to valuation, concentration, and complexity risks (and may be subject to elevated spread risk, particularly in stressed conditions).[/li][/ul][/ml]The report identifies important guardrails in the (re)insurance market for asset-intensive products to mitigate risks and ensure prudence in the asset-intensive reinsurance market. It outlines the complex nature of reinsurance supervision, a global industry requiring regulators to collaborate across jurisdictions. It highlights collateral structures in the form of modified coinsurance and funds withheld as key protective features of asset-intensive reinsurance. In light of the evolving landscape and associated challenges, the BMA explains that it has implemented a tailored supervisory regime, with enhancements that include prior regulatory approval for long-term block reinsurance transactions and updated capital requirements. 

[b]What's next? [/b]The BMA views reinsurance as a proven tool for risk management and can enhance the protection of and access to insurance by increasing the capacity, diversification, and stability of the insurance sector and enabling insurers to offer more affordable and suitable products and services to customers. Possibly related to the NAIC's recent initiatives to revise actuarial standards and reporting related to asset-intensive reinsurance transactions, the BMA makes clear its support of initiatives by ceding jurisdictions to have insurers demonstrate adequate risk management to identify, measure, monitor, manage, and report the risks to which they are exposed.

resources_with_topic.Name: Non-Bond Debt & Schedule BA Assets
Proposed Enhancements to Public Disclosure Regime: Public Disclosure of Assets and Liabilities for Commercial Long-term Insurers
resources_with_topic.notes:
[color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]BMA Proposes Powerful Market Oversight Through Public Disclosure of Life & Annuity Investments[/color]

[color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]Aligned with its ongoing efforts to enhance transparency, the Bermuda Monetary Authority (BMA) posted a request for comment on [/color][color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://cdn.bma.bm/documents/2024-12-23-16-49-15-Consultation-Paper---Proposed-Enhancements-to-Public-Disclosure-Regime---Public-Disclosure-of-Assets-and-Liabilities-for-Commercial-Long-term-Insurers.pdf][i]Proposed Enhancements to Public Disclosure Regime: Public Disclosure of Assets and Liabilities for Commercial Long-term Insurers[/i][/url][/color][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]. The proposed public disclosure of assets and liabilities responds to the broader global trend and structural shifts in the life and annuity sector, which has seen an increased appetite for illiquid, hard-to-value assets that are not publicly traded and can be more complex than liquid traded assets. The [/color][color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://cdn.bma.bm/documents/2024-12-30-10-04-08-Template-for-Public-Disclosure-of-Assets-and-Liabilities.xlsm]Template for Public Disclosure of Assets and Liabilities[/url][/color][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)] would have insurers provide instrument-level (i.e., by asset CUSIP/ISIN/International ID) public disclosure of long-term assets, along with disclosure of liability profiles in a way that intends to allow for a public assessment of investments and their appropriateness. This level of disclosure aligns the BMA more closely with NAIC reporting guidelines and provides what we view as a powerful market oversight mechanism. The proposal is closely linked to the recent [/color][color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://www.bma.bm/viewPDF/documents/2024-12-11-11-27-51-Consultation-Paper---Proposed-Instructions-and-Guidanceon-the-Application-of-the-Prudent-Person-Principle.pdf][i]Proposed Instructions and Guidance on the Application of the Prudent Person Principle[/i][/url][/color][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)], which requires that an insurer only assume investment risks that it can properly identify, measure, respond to, monitor, control, and report on while considering its capital requirements and adequacy, short-term and long-term liquidity requirements, and policyholder obligations (see [/color][color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://mailchi.mp/bridgewayanalytics/newsreel-17315081?e=9ea3ec665c]ART Newsreel | December 5, 2024[/url][/color][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]). [/color]

[b][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]What's next?[/color][/b][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)] The request for comment is open through February 28, 2025. The BMA intends the new requirements to become effective on December 31, 2025, with a voluntary opt-in starting December 31, 2024.[/color]

Assessment of Climate-Related Vulnerabilities Analytical Framework and Toolkit
resources_with_topic.notes:
[color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]The Financial Stability Board (FSB) published its [/color][color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://www.fsb.org/uploads/P160125.pdf][i]Assessment of Climate-Related Vulnerabilities Analytical Framework and Toolkit[/i][/url][/color][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)] on January 16, 2025. The FSB, which includes the U.S. Federal Reserve, Treasury, the Securities and Exchange Commission, and representatives from G20 economies, was formed in 2009 to coordinate national financial authorities and international standard-setting bodies to develop strong regulatory, supervisory, and other financial sector policies.[/color]

[color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]The report assesses climate-related financial vulnerabilities in the global financial system, aiming to address financial stability risks arising from interconnected physical and transition climate risks. It outlines how climate shocks (e.g., extreme weather or policy shifts) interact with existing financial vulnerabilities (e.g., asset mispricing, high leverage) to amplify risks. Key channels include credit, market, liquidity, and underwriting risks. [/color]

[color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]The toolkit is comprised of three main categories of metrics: (1) Proxies to signal potential climate risk drivers, (2) Exposure metrics to provide insights into how risks propagate through the financial system, and (3) Risk metrics to quantify financial impacts on institutions and systems.[/color]

[color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]To demonstrate the framework’s application, the report examines the potential financial stability consequences of a physical climate risk via real estate markets to demonstrate how the framework can be applied in practice. The analysis involves a severe yet arguably plausible conceptual scenario of how a physical climate shock to the real estate sector may affect financial stability if insurance becomes less available, which causes risks to shift to households, businesses, or governments.[/color]

[b][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]What's next? [/color][/b][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]The report outlines the FSB's continued development and operationalization of the framework and toolkit, focusing on refining metrics, addressing data gaps, and advancing analytical capabilities. The FSB will rely on the analytical work of member authorities and international organizations, such as the International Association of Insurance Supervisors (IAIS) and the International Organization of Securities Commissions (IOSCO), to advance work on the metrics.[/color]

resources_with_topic.Name: FSB Roadmap for Addressing Climate-related Financial Risks
U.K. Prudential Regulatory Authority (PRA)'s January 9, 2025, Letter to Chief Executive Officers of U.K. Insurers
resources_with_topic.notes:
The U.K. Prudential Regulatory Authority (PRA)'s [color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://www.bankofengland.co.uk/-/media/boe/files/prudential-regulation/letter/2025/insurance-supervision-2025-priorities.pdf]January 9, 2025, Letter to Chief Executive Officers of U.K. Insurers[/url][/color] outlines the sector's 2025 thematic priorities. With the concluding year-end 2024 Solvency II review and reinstatement of assimilated law into the PRA Rulebook and other policy materials, the PRA completed all the steps necessary to implement U.K. Solvency, which initiated with Brexit. 

In 2025, the PRA priorities include spurring a vibrant, innovative, and internationally competitive insurance sector and supporting firms to make long-term investments to support growth. To this end:
[ml][ul][li indent=0 align=left]The PRA has set up a new Matching Adjustment (MA) permissions team, which allows for assessing MA applications more quickly and facilitates the broader range of investment opportunities available within the MA portfolio. The new MA guidelines allow U.K. firms more flexibility by expanding the set of assets given preferential treatment through ALM initiatives. In 2025, it will explore other steps to support insurers take advantage of the reforms, including the new MA Investment Accelerator. [/li][li indent=0 align=left]The PRA has implemented a new ‘mobilization’ regime within its New Insurers Startup Unit, designed to support potential new insurers seeking to establish themselves in the U.K.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]The PRA has streamlined authorizing and supervising third-country branches.[/li][/ul][/ml]
The PRA is also taking on several other initiatives that are tied to competitiveness and growth:
[ml][ul][li indent=0 align=left]The bulk purchase annuity (BPA) market has grown rapidly in the U.K., given the continued high demand for corporate-defined pension scheme risk transfer solutions. The structure and complexity of BPA transactions have been evolving with demand, and the PRA expects firms' risk management and control frameworks to keep up.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]Funded (i.e., asset-intensive) reinsurance is an area of focus, given that its growth could, if not adequately controlled, lead to a rapid buildup of risks in the sector and have the potential to pose systemic risks. The letter clarifies that firms’ self-assessments show they are not fully meeting supervisory expectations.[/li][/ul][/ml]
The PRA is looking to improve its ability to evaluate and maintain resilience:
[ml][ul][li indent=0 align=left]The Life Insurance Stress Test (LIST) will be launched in January 2025 and will provide insights into firm-level financial resilience for the first time for roughly a dozen insurers. The PRA also intends to publish aggregate results and provide additional context to support transparency and enhance understanding of how firms’ financial positions evolve under stress.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]Liquidity resilience is another area of focus, partly motivated by the market liquidity stresses in recent years, including the ‘Dash for Cash’ and the LDI crisis. The PRA plans to improve liquidity reporting, encouraging relevant insurers to sign up for the Bank of England’s new collateralized loan facility for non-banks and follow up on their thematic review of insurers’ liquidity risk management.[/li][/ul][/ml]

resources_with_topic.Name: BMA 2025 Business Plan
resources_with_topic.notes:
The Bermuda Monetary Authority (BMA) published its [color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://cdn.bma.bm/documents/2025-01-15-12-03-36-BMA-2025-Business-Plan.pdf][i]2025 Business Plan[/i][/url][/color]. For insurers, the plan includes focusing on:
[ml][ul][li indent=0 align=left]Continued participation in the International Association of Insurance Supervisors (IAIS) initiatives, including embedding the Common Framework for Internationally Active Insurance Groups and the Holistic Framework for Systemic Risk into the Bermuda commercial regulatory regime.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]Further integrating climate and sustainability into the regulatory and supervisory frameworks, focusing on climate change and protection gaps.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]Providing further guidance on applying the prudent person regime for investment management.  [/li][li indent=0 align=left]Proposing enhanced public disclosure requirements on investments for long-term[/li][li indent=0 align=left]commercial insurers.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]Considering enhancements to our treatment of structured products and loans.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]Proposing legislative enhancements to provide for additional insurer management and governance accountability.[/li][/ul][/ml][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]The Business Plan aligns very well with the BMA's significant efforts to revise its governance framework and enhance transparency under Bermuda's shifting business landscape.[/color]

resources_with_topic.Name: Analyses of U.S. Homeowners Insurance Markets, 2018-2022: Climate-Related Risks and Other Factors
resources_with_topic.notes:
The U.S. Department of the Treasury Federal Insurance Office (FIO) published its January 2025 report, [color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://home.treasury.gov/system/files/311/Analyses_of_US_Homeowners_Insurance_Markets_2018-2022_Climate-Related_Risks_and_Other_Factors_0.pdf]Analyses of U.S. Homeowners Insurance Markets, 2018-2022: Climate-Related Risks and Other Factors[/url][/color]. The Report analyzes a new dataset created through a collaboration among the NAIC, state insurance regulators, and FIO, whereby the NAIC has collected data to assess climate-related financial risks in insurance. The dataset represents approximately 80% of premiums written by insurers for an average of 49 million private homeowners policies annually in the U.S., aggregated at the ZIP Code level. The Report distinguishes [i]climate-related disasters[/i], such as hurricanes and wildfires, from non-weather-related natural phenomena, such as earthquakes and earthquake-related tsunamis. From 2018 through 2022—the five-year period for the data analyzed in this Report—the annual number of major disaster declarations for climate-related events was almost double the average over the 50-year period from 1960 to 2010.

[b]A few key findings that caught our eye:[/b]
[ml][ul][li indent=0 align=left]Between 2018 and 2022, average premiums increased 8.7% faster than inflation, with an increase of 14.7% in the top 20% of ZIP Codes and a decline of at least 1.4% in the lowest 20%.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]From a regional perspective, different predominant climate-related perils resulted in diverse claims and loss patterns. Hurricanes accounted for more expected annual losses to buildings than other perils in the United States. They were associated with the highest premiums per policy and nonrenewal rates in the seven U.S. regions.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]Factors driving these trends include inflation in building costs, shifts in population to high-risk areas, rising reinsurance costs, and regulatory and litigation-related dynamics, further compounded by increasing disaster frequency and severity.[/li][/ul][/ml]
[color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]FIO plans to continue collaborating with state insurance regulators, the NAIC, and other stakeholders to monitor and address challenges in the U.S. homeowners insurance market. The FIO recommendations include enhancing data collection, proactive risk mitigation, and coordinated efforts among federal, state, and private stakeholders to address affordability and accessibility challenges in high-risk regions. The Treasury is releasing a subset of the aggregated[/color]
[color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]data metrics underlying the analysis in the Report on its [/color][color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://home.treasury.gov/policy-issues/financial-markets-financial-institutions-and-fiscal-service/federal-insurance-office/reports-notices]website[/url][/color][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)].[/color]

resources_with_topic.Name: Evaluation of the Effects of the G20 Financial Regulatory Reforms on Securitization
resources_with_topic.notes:
[b]FSB Concludes Securitization Reforms Contributed to Resilience Without Impairing Credit Supply. [/b]The FSB published its final [color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://www.fsb.org/2025/01/evaluation-of-the-effects-of-the-g20-financial-regulatory-reforms-on-securitisation-final-report/][i]Evaluation of the Effects of the G20 Financial Regulatory Reforms on Securitisation[/i][/url][/color][i],[/i] concluding reforms after the global financial crisis (GFC) have contributed positively to the resilience of securitization markets, particularly for residential mortgage-backed securities (RMBS), without significantly impairing credit supply to the real economy. The FSB had previously published a request for comment on its preliminary report, [color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://www.fsb.org/wp-content/uploads/P020724.pdf][i]Evaluation of the Effects of the G20 Financial Regulatory Reforms on Securitization[/i][/url][/color].

[b]﻿Why does the report matter?[/b]
[ml][ul][li indent=0 align=left]The conclusions are contentious, with many [color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://www.fsb.org/uploads/P220125-2.pdf]respondents arguing[/url][/color] that the reforms impeded E.U. securitization market development compared to the U.S., which did not implement comparable reforms. While the FSB is not a regulator, its mandate includes promoting policies it views as providing financial stability and encouraging coherent policy implementation across jurisdictions. The conclusions of the final study mean that those advocating revised treatment of securitizations, particularly in the E.U. and the U.K., will find the hill evermore steep.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]Many aspects are relevant to the NAIC's effort to categorize, assess the credit risk of, and refine the capital treatment of CLOs and other asset-backed securities (ABS).[/li][/ul][/ml]
[b]The Details[/b]. The report focuses on the International Organization of Securities Commissions (IOSCO) minimum retention recommendations and the Basel Committee on Banking Supervision (BCBS) revisions to prudential requirements for bank securitization-related exposures in global RMBS and CLO markets, markets, with particular attention to U.S., E.U., U.K., and emerging markets. The objectives of the FSB report are twofold:
[ml][ol][li indent=0 align=left]To assess the extent to which the G20 reforms have achieved their financial stability objectives.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]To examine broader effects (positive or negative) of the reforms on securitization markets and their implications for the real economy, which will help identify unintended consequences.[/li][/ol][/ml]While the analysis and discussions offer much to learn, one should be cautious when interpreting the conclusions given the nuanced nature of the data, which the report acknowledges. The risk retention and bank securitization requirements aim to reduce misaligned incentives and promote sound markets, but neither is directly observable, and the evaluation examines the mechanisms through which the reforms are expected to operate. These include the complexity and opaqueness of structures, credit enhancement, changes in the investor base, credit quality of underlying loans and credit performance, pricing of securitized assets, and the robustness of these markets during recent episodes of stress and in hypothetical scenario analyses.

[b]IOSCO minimum retention recommendations[/b]. The report points to existing studies that find that underlying loans of securitization deals with risk retention have a lower probability of becoming non-performing, lower loan-to-value (LTV) ratios, higher income-to-debt service ratios, a lower delinquency amount, and a shorter time in arrears, relative to a control group of securitized loans without risk retention.

[b]BCBS revisions to bank securitization requirements[/b]. The report points to the growth of securitizations classified under BCBS as simple, transparent, and comparable (STC), which receive favorable bank capital treatment. It argues that the market pricing for STS transactions in the E.U. generally shows lower spreads than non-STS transactions, likely reflecting investor perception of lower risk. The report concludes that reforms improving resilience have been achieved, in part, through a redistribution of risk from banks to the non-bank financial intermediation (NBFI) sector, with banks shifting towards higher-rated tranches. This redistribution of risk has been driven both by an increase in non-bank financing of the economy and by the growth of non-bank investors in securitizations.

[b]The broader effects of the reforms on securitization markets and financing the real economy. [/b]The report points to previous evaluations by the FSB and BCBS that have found:
[ml][ul][li indent=0 align=left]Gains in banking sector resilience from Basel III, particularly for banks more heavily impacted by the reforms;[/li][li indent=0 align=left]While the reforms may have limited lending by banks with weaker initial regulatory ratios, there is no indication that the reforms impaired the aggregate supply of credit to the economy;[/li][li indent=0 align=left]These reforms helped shield the global banking sector and real economy from a more severe crisis during the March 2023 banking turmoil.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]Other non-regulatory factors constrain the growth of E.U. securitizations, including the interplay between low supply and weak demand due to a lack of inherent interest from both sides. The report points to originators having cheaper funding sources such as covered bonds, deposits, and ECB repos, and a perception of securitization as a complex product with extensive due diligence requirements remains with investors. A further factor is that most E.U. securitization is done with national-level collateral, resulting in smaller, less liquid, and fragmented markets.[/li][/ul][/ml]
[b]The FSB hosted a supporting [/b][url=https://www.fsb.org/2024/07/virtual-public-workshop-on-the-evaluation-of-the-effects-of-the-g20-financial-regulatory-reforms-on-securitisation/][b]Workshop on July 19, 2024[/b][/url][b],[/b] to receive feedback from participants. Discussions from the panel and commenters, which included market and academic stakeholders, highlighted the strong views on the report's conclusions and the challenge of attributing particular market outcomes to the reforms due to confounding factors such as the low interest rate, environment, and many other reforms taking place at the same time. They also highlighted strong and varied views on how securitization markets should be governed and the undesirable consequences of the current framework (paraphrased):
[ml][ul][li indent=0 align=left][b]General comments, not limited to risk-retention and bank securitization requirtments[/b].[/li][ul data=1][li indent=1 align=left][b]Incongruis rules and disjoint markets[/b]. The rules have been implemented differently across jurisdictions. For example, risk retention rules apply to CLOs in Europe but not in the U.S., which the report discusses extensively. It highlights that risk retention is much lower for U.S. CLOs, which is not surprising. There is a wide spectrum of non-overlapping common and incongruent guidelines across jurisdictions. The different rules have resulted in markets evolving considerably and differently across jurisdictions and local and fragmented securitization markets, which was argued to be undesirable.[/li][li indent=1 align=left][b]It is not possible to attribute capital market resilience to specific reforms. [/b]Panelists highlighted that a broad set of reforms has contributed to more stable capital markets since the GFC. Mortgage underwriting standards, for example, have improved broadly and significantly, leading to a more robust RMBS market. Commenters pointed to the risk of arriving at a misleading conclusion of a causal relationship rather than a correlated relationship with the role of the BCBS and IOSCO reforms in improving market stability.[/li][li indent=1 align=left][b]De-banking[/b]. Since the GFC, there has been a general shift from the banking sector to the non-banking sector. The shift to the nonbank sector is not unique to securitization, as various factors and structural changes have increased reliance on market-based implementation. One panelist pointed to the trend of nonbanks seeking bank registration, particularly in the European auto sector, highlighting how capital markets evolve with the need for capital and regulatory restrictions.[/li][li indent=1 align=left][b]There is a need to consider asset classes holistically[/b]. Several commenters pointed to similarities between CLO and other transactions, such as Significant Risk Transfers (SRT), common in Europe, and the need for a consistent set of rules. Several commenters referenced debt mutual funds and Business Development Companies (BDCs) as points of reference for divergent treatment. Deliberations had elements similar to those related to the principles-based bond definition and the NAIC's categorization of BDCs, which the SEC places limits on its leverage as an operating company and CLOs as ABS.[/li][li indent=1 align=left][b]Markets have evolved considerably since the GFC[/b]. The current guidelines were designed based on the important lessons from the GFC, and markets have evolved considerably since then. The development of the private debt market is sizable and now competes with the CLO market as a source of capital. This is important from a policy perspective, considering the implications of capital flows and restrictions on various forms of securities and the possibility of inadvertently introducing fragility into the system.[/li][li indent=1 align=left][b]Securitization plays a critical role in financial markets[/b]. The rules can significantly impact capital flows and risk concentration, which was generally acknowledged. Important considerations for the downstream implications of the cost of borrowing across market segments, which have socioeconomic and political considerations, were also noted.[/li][/ul][li indent=0 align=left][b]Risk-retention[/b]. Panel members had varied views on risk retention rules' impact and significance:[/li][ul data=1][li indent=1 align=left][b]Markets evolve to achieve a level of comfort with transactions[/b]. One panel member pointed out that risk retention was not mandated but practiced prior to the GFC, and after the GFC, it was mandated inconsistently, and markets have evolved in ways of ensuring participants achieve a level of comfort with transactions. However, we caution against taking that argument too far, given the complex nature of securitizations and the possible perverse incentives, with obvious examples that lead to the GFC.[/li][li indent=1 align=left][b]Risk-retention is only one mechanism[/b]. Another panelist pointed out that risk retention is only one narrow rule receiving more attention than it should. Another pointed out other mechanisms to align interests, such as underwriting the transparency disclosure requirements. For CLOs, for example, the manager's compensation creates incentives similar to risk-retention rules.[/li][li indent=1 align=left][b]The CLO and collateral loan market have unique qualities[/b]. The underlying loans are syndicated with a significant level of transparency, with management compensation having similar properties to risk-retention, which was argued to make risk-retention less relevant. The syndicated loan market was argued to have a unique market oversight feature with significant analysis of collateral loans by a significant number of sophisticated institutional players in the syndicated market. A familiar discussion ensued on whether the resiliency of the CLO market has been tested, mirroring the discussions in the NAIC CLO Ad-hoc Technical Group, which is exploring NAIC model-based Designations. [/li][/ul][li indent=0 align=left][b]Bank securitization requirements[/b]. The report points to the growth of securitizations classified under BCBS as simple, transparent, and comparable (STC), which receive favorable bank capital treatment, arguing that the market pricing for STC transactions in the E.U. generally shows lower spreads than non-STC transactions,[/li][ul data=1][li indent=1 align=left][b]Capital charges[/b]. Several commenters pointed to the need to assess the capital charge in conjunction with the STC classification since the classification alone does not provide a complete story on treatment and its impact on investment incentives. This is a familiar theme in the context of recent efforts by the NAIC to classify and differentiate the capital of ABS residuals and equity.[/li][li indent=1 align=left][b]Lower spreads for STC transactions[/b]. The report observes that STC transactions in the E.U. generally have lower spreads, which is attributed to investors' perception of lower risk and (in the case of banks and insurers) reduced capital requirements. It also notes the possibility that introducing the STC category has led to relabelling some transactions rather than stimulating activity in the securitization market, potentially reflecting the underlying structural differences already present in the market. Panelists quickly pointed out that the lower spreads of STC transactions are a natural byproduct of their characteristics.[/li][/ul][/ul][/ml]
[b]Why does this matter? [/b]While the FSB is not a regulator, its mandate includes promoting policies it views as providing financial stability and encouraging coherent policy implementation across jurisdictions. The analysis thus far suggests that the reforms, of which there are clearly many critics, have contributed to the resilience of the securitization market without strong evidence of material negative side effects on financing the economy. If the final study arrives at the same conclusions, those advocating revised treatment of securitizations, particularly in Europe and the U.K., will find the hill evermore steep.

resources_with_topic.Name: Restricted Assets
resources_with_topic.notes:
[justify]Most insurers have restricted assets, facing limitations on their use or disposition due to contractual, regulatory, or legal obligations. Examples include assets that are lent or posted as collateral in securities lending transactions, funds held under reinsurance agreements, or pledged on deposit with state regulators. In many cases, but not always, these assets are identified separately in statutory financial statements and often require additional disclosures and RBC. Details related to conforming securities lending transactions, for example, are reported under Schedule DL, ensuring the impact of restrictions on liquidity and solvency are understood and face an additional RBC charge of 0.2% for life and property & casualty companies. Several NAIC initiatives may impact the classification, reporting, RBC charges, and use of certain restricted assets, with potential downstream implications for investment strategies - welcome to this week's [i]In the Weeds[/i]![/justify]
[justify][/justify]
[justify][b]Under Current Guidelines. [/b]Investments classified as [i]not under insurers' exclusive control[/i] are identified using a restricted access code. Those investments are then grouped and reported under categories that receive differentiated RBC charges. Life investments, categorized as not exclusively in their control, constituted roughly $526 billion in year-end 2023 ($199 billion for property & casualty): [/justify]
[ml][ul][li indent=0 align=justify]Loaned or leased[/li][li indent=0 align=justify]Subject to repurchase and reverse repurchase agreements[/li][li indent=0 align=justify]Pledged as collateral, excluding federal home loan bank (FHLB) transactions[/li][li indent=0 align=justify]Pledged as collateral in FHLB transactions[/li][li indent=0 align=justify]Pledged under an option agreement[/li][li indent=0 align=justify]Letter stock or otherwise restricted sale[/li][li indent=0 align=justify]FHLB stock (which cannot be sold to a third party)[/li][li indent=0 align=justify]Pledged on deposit with state or other regulatory bodies[/li][li indent=0 align=justify]Restricted for multiple or other reasons[/li][/ul][/ml][justify]Aggregate holdings are reported and face an additional off-balance-sheet charge for collateral risk that is not otherwise captured in the RBC formula, falling under C-0 for life (R-0 for property & casualty) companies: [/justify]
[ml][ul][li indent=0 align=justify][b]Securities lending[/b]. Life companies lent $36 billion of securities in 2023 (property & casualty lent $12 billion). Transactions are subject to an RBC charge of 0.2% when part of a conforming program and are otherwise subject to the pre-2021 RBC NAIC Designation 2 (~BBB rating) charge of 1.26% for life companies (1% for property & casualty).[/li][li indent=0 align=justify][b]Repurchase agreements[/b]. In 2023, life investments restricted by (reverse) repurchase agreements amounted to $42 billion ($2 billion for property & casualty), and are subject to an RBC charge of 1.26% (1% for property & casualty); more on this in a moment.[/li][li indent=0 align=justify][b]Letter stock or securities restricted as to sale. [/b]Life companies held $11 billion of sale-restricted securities in 2023 (property & casualty held $18 billion), receiving an RBC charge of 1.26% (1% for property & casualty), of which $7 billion was FHLB stock ($0.4 billion FHLB stock was held by property & casualty).[/li][li indent=0 align=justify][b]Deposited with regulators. [/b]Life companies deposited $11 billion with regulators in 2023 (property & casualty deposited $65 billion), receiving an RBC charge of 1.26% (1% for property & casualty), of which $4 billion was with state insurance regulators (property & casualty deposited $54 billion with state insurance regulators).[/li][li indent=0 align=justify][b]Pledged as collateral – excluding collateral pledged to an FHLB. [/b]Life companies pledged $122 billion in collateral (property & casualty pledged $24 billion). Collateral receives an RBC charge of 1.26% (1% for property & casualty), other than life collateral to derivative counterparties or central clearinghouses for initial margin and variation margin. Life derivative collateral receives a 0.39% charge, the pre-2021 RBC C-1 NAIC Designation 1 charge (~AAA - A rating). [/li][li indent=0 align=justify][b]Pledged as collateral to FHLB – including assets backing funding agreements[/b]. Life companies pledged $239 billion (property & casualty pledged $17 billion) in collateral with FHLBs in 2023. Life companies only face an RBC charge on the minimum required FHLB collateral amount since the insurer can recall the excess at any time. In addition, collateral covered by RBC C-3 Phase I Cash Flow Testing is excluded since provisions associated with FHLB liabilities are taken into account. The minimum required FHLB collateral not subject to the C-3 Phase I Cash Flow Testing will generally align with the Designation assigned to FHLB unsecured debt. If FHLB-funded advances exceed 5% of total net admitted assets, the RBC factor is the blended rate such that collateral supporting FHLB funding agreement liabilities in excess of the limit is subject to the 1.26% factor.[/li][li indent=0 align=justify][b]Placed under option agreements[/b]. Life companies placed $1.7 billion under option agreements in 2023 (property & casualty placed $0.1 billion), receiving an RBC charge of 1.26% (1% for property & casualty). [/li][li indent=0 align=justify][b]Other[/b].[b] [/b]Life companies classified $63 billion as other in 2023 (property & casualty classified $62 billion), receiving an RBC charge of 1.26% (1% for property & casualty).[/li][/ul][/ml][justify][/justify]
[justify][b]Possible Changes and Implications. [/b]The Statutory Accounting Principles (E) Working Group (SAPWG) is considering two potential changes to the disclosure and treatment of restricted assets supporting: (1) Modified coinsurance (MODCO) or funds withheld (FWH) reinsurance transactions, and (2) Repurchase and reverse repurchase agreements.[/justify]
[justify][/justify]
[justify][b]Assets supporting MODCO or FWH reinsurance transactions [/b]([color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://content.naic.org/sites/default/files/inline-files/24-20%20-%20Restricted%20Asset%20Clarification.docx]SAPWG#2024-20[/url][/color] and [color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://content.naic.org/sites/default/files/inline-files/24-07%20-%20Modco%20Reporting_0.docx]SAPWG#2024-07[/url][/color] last discussed in [color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://mailchi.mp/bridgewayanalytics/newsreel-17314594?e=9ea3ec665c]ART Newsreel | November 21, 2024[/url][/color]). Under MODCO or FWH transactions, the ceding insurer retains control of supporting assets, which, in some cases, are placed in a trust. NAIC staff observed reporting inconsistencies across filing schedules and variations in reporting standards across insurers, which could significantly impact the financial ratios and RBC of these transactions. NAIC staff has flagged several issues: (1) Insurers receive an RBC offset for assets that support these transactions, (2) The fair value of the assets withheld is reported in the reinsurance Schedules, (3) There are no clear guidelines on identifying the assets, with some insurers assigning them a restricted access code, (4) Reporting guidelines for non-controlling assets in the general interrogatories, which flow into RBC calculations, can be different from reporting guidelines for restricted assets elsewhere, and (5) There are no restrictions on using assets under a MODCO or FWH agreement as collateral or as a pledged asset for a purpose unrelated to the reinsurance agreement (e.g., FHLB transaction). NAIC staff recommends several changes:[/justify]
[ml][ul][li indent=0 align=justify][b]Reporting[/b]. [color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://content.naic.org/sites/default/files/inline-files/24-07%20-%20Modco%20Reporting_0.docx]SAPWG#2024-07[/url][/color] recommends adding a new part to reinsurance Schedules that would allow for identifying assets held under FWH and MODCO transactions, similar in structure to Schedule DL, which covers securities lending. [color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://content.naic.org/sites/default/files/inline-files/24-20%20-%20Restricted%20Asset%20Clarification.docx]SAPWG#2024-20[/url][/color] proposes revisions to restricted asset disclosure in Note 5L to more clearly identify how MODCO and FWH assets are reported. SAPWG proposes sponsoring a Blanks proposal incorporating Annual Statement instruction revisions clarifying aggregate restricted assets to ensure the restricted asset disclosure is consistently reported. [/li][li indent=0 align=justify][b]RBC[/b]. [color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://content.naic.org/sites/default/files/inline-files/24-20%20-%20Restricted%20Asset%20Clarification.docx]SAPWG#2024-20[/url][/color] does not capture MODCO or FWH assets in the restricted asset reporting that flows through to the general interrogatories, which results in additional “noncontrolled” asset RBC. The proposal would capture MODCO and FWH assets in the existing restricted asset disclosure (SSAP No. 1, paragraph 23c) on collateral when a corresponding liability is reported, providing a complete view of restricted assets with no additional RBC impact. SAPWG is also sending the Life Risk-Based Capital (E) Working Group (LRBCWG) a referral to clarify that MODCO assets held by a ceding entity that are pledged or used by the ceding entity for other purposes, such as FHLB transactions, are not permitted to be reported as an RBC credit.[/li][/ul][/ml][justify][/justify]
[justify][b]Repurchase and Reverse Repurchase Agreements. [/b]Last discussed in [color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://mailchi.mp/bridgewayanalytics/newsreel-17311203?e=9ea3ec665c]ART Newsreel | August 8, 2024[/url][/color], repurchase agreements (repos) are increasingly popular with life companies, and the Life Risk-Based Capital (E) Working Group (LRBCWG) is considering a proposal from the ACLI (SPAWG#2024-04, [color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://content.naic.org/sites/default/files/national_meeting/Agendas-SAPWG-Meeting-8-13-24.pdf]Agenda Page 11[/url][/color] and [color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://content.naic.org/sites/default/files/national_meeting/Materials-SAPWG-Hearing-8-13-24.pdf]Attachments 13 & 14[/url][/color]) to modify the life RBC formula to mirror the treatment of conforming security lending arrangements; securities lent are subject to a 0.2% RBC charge when part of a conforming program, while repos are subject to a 1.26% charge. As part of the process, LRBCWG referred the proposal to SAPWG for feedback on the accounting and reporting aspects. SAPWG identified that the statutory accounting and reporting for securities lending and repurchase agreements are currently different (e.g., securities lending collateral is detailed in Schedule DL, which does not include repurchase agreement collateral). [/justify]
[justify] [/justify]
[justify]NAIC staff memo [color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://content.naic.org/sites/default/files/inline-files/24-04%20-%20Sec%20Lending%20%26%20Repo%20Memo.docx][i]Discussion of Security Lending / Repo Reporting Includes Restricted Assets Reporting & RBC Impacts[/i][/url][/color] details accounting and reporting guidance for repos and securities lending transactions. Under a repo agreement, insurers sell their securities to another party with a commitment to repurchase the securities at a later date. In contrast, insurers lend their security in securities lending transactions. These secured short-term transactions are considered useful low-risk yield-generating strategies (see NAIC Capital Markets Bureau report [color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://content.naic.org/sites/default/files/capital-markets-special-reports-securities-lending-repo-ye2021.pdf][i]U.S. Insurance Industry’s Exposure to Securities Lending and Repurchase Agreements[/i][/url][/color]). Cash or collateral on the balance sheet is treated like any other insurer asset in reporting, valuation, and RBC. Initially, both transactions received a C-0 of 1.26%, but in 2006, C-0 was revised to 0.20% for conforming securities lending programs. The ACLI proposes that conforming repo programs receive the favorable 0.20% C-0 charge. It includes heightened disclosure, an additional RBC charge applied to the 2% “Overcollateralization Amount,” which is argued to be akin to the unsecured credit exposures insurers assume in their bond portfolios. [/justify]
[justify] [/justify]
[justify][b]What's next? [/b]Both sets of initiatives are on [color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://content.naic.org/sites/default/files/inline-files/Maintenance%20Agenda%20-%20As%20of%2011172024.xlsx][i]SAPWG's Maintenance Agenda[/i][/url][/color], and we expect regulators to resume deliberations once the group reconstitutes.[/justify]
