The Significant Trends in Reinsurance, ModCo & FWH Transactions
resources_with_topic.notes:
Proposed changes to the treatment of assets supporting asset-intensive reinsurance transactions are significant, and the investment community should closely monitor their implications for investment strategies. The challenge, in part, is the dizzying forms of reinsurance transactions and identifying which transactions might be impacted, how they might be impacted (e.g., through reserving and surplus, reporting, or restricted use of assets), and the materiality of the changes. What topic can be more appropriate for this week's [i]In the Weeds[/i]? Let's dive in!

[b]Some context on reinsurance and concerns.[/b] The Bermuda Monetary Authority (BMA) September 2024 report, [url=https://cdn.bma.bm/documents/2024-09-06-12-10-21-Bermuda-Long-Term-Insurance-Collateral-Structures.pdf][i]Collateral Structures in the Bermuda Long-Term Insurance Market[/i][/url], provides a helpful frame of the potential concerns. [i]Reinsurance acts as a risk mitigation tool in the insurance industry that spreads risk to multiple counterparties, diversifying risks borne by insurers and bringing in additional capital to support those risks. Life and annuity reinsurance (long-term reinsurance) of the asset-intensive type can transform risks for the cedent by moving most key risks such as insurance, market, and credit risks to the reinsurer and substituting them with reinsurer counterparty credit risk. Thus, the concern for the cedent shifts from financial and insurance risks to the ability of the reinsurer to fulfill its promises over the total runoff of the liabilities or such other period as contractually defined.[/i]

[b]To help mitigate counterparty risk in reinsurance transactions[/b], reinsurance transactions are structured with various contractual provisions that ensure the ceding company can recover amounts due even if the reinsurer defaults. These transactions often include funds withheld (FWH) arrangements, collateral trusts, letters of credit, and guarantees, allowing insurers to maintain financial flexibility and ensure claims obligations are met.

[b]The NAIC has been refining the rules governing reinsurance transactions.[/b] The regulatory toolbox now includes Actuarial Guidelines LIII (AG 53), and there are ongoing initiatives to heighten disclosure (for a review of efforts under the Statutory Accounting Principles (E) Working Group (SAPWG), see [url=https://mailchi.mp/bridgewayanalytics/newsreel-17314594?e=9ea3ec665c&mc_cid=03096abd4d]ART Newsreel | November 21, 2024[/url]). In addition, the Life Actuarial (A) Task Force (LATF) is proposing significant enhancements to reserving when life companies enter into reinsurance transactions (see [url=https://#Reinsurance]Assets in Reinsurance Transactions[/url]). The challenge regulators face with oversing reinsurance is driven by complexities from the significant variation in reinsurance and the lack of transparency with cross-border transactions, which are generally reported under accounting and regulatory frameworks that are not comparable to statutory accounting and Risk-Based Capital. In addition, U.S. insurers are not required to identify which assets support reinsurance transactions, which SAPWG is looking to rectify. 

[b]How significant are the trends? Let's break down a few headline numbers for U.S. life companies' general account in year-end 2023:[/b]
[ml][ul][li indent=0 align=left]$5,347 billion — Net Admitted Cash & Invested Assets.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]$4,080 billion — Aggregate Reserves (which is net of Modified Coinsurance (ModCo) Reserves, which we'll discuss shortly). For those less familiar with statutory accounting, reserves are reported as liabilities and represent earmarked assets needed to support the estimated future obligations for policyholder claims, benefits, and related expenses.[/li][li indent=0 align=left][b]$1,333 billion — Reserve credit for ceded reinsurance, [/b]representing the reduction in reserves that the ceding insurer can take because the responsibility for the associated future obligations has been transferred to the reinsurer. This credit reflects that the ceding insurer no longer needs to hold assets to support these obligations, as they are now the reinsurer's liability.[/li][/ul][/ml]The headline number is significant and worth digging deeper into its components. The NAIC and other rulemaking bodies have highlighted the growth in offshore reinsurance, including affiliated transactions, as a trend that needs to be better understood. The trend is striking, with Non-U.S. transactions reaching $466 billion in 2023 ($271 billion — Affiliated and $195 billion — Unaffiliated) and Unaffiliated Non-U.S. transactions experiencing the highest growth rate.

[center][img width=500]https://mcusercontent.com/79c3cb5ae113df9ea96a5e443/images/ef5ab874-3f9e-fb33-4358-880294c9075b.png[/img][/center]
[center][/center]
[b]ModCo and FWH transactions, and assets supporting reinsurance transactions. [/b]As discussed above, reinsurance transactions often include FWH arrangements to, in part, help mitigate counterparty risk, where a portion of the assets supporting the reinsured business remains with the ceding insurer rather than being transferred to the reinsurer. This structure limits the reinsurer’s ability to take on a more aggressive capital position or investment strategy related to the reinsured block.

ModCo and Coinsurance FWH have been receiving significant regulatory scrutiny, with proposed changes that could impact the treatment of supporting investments and reserve credit calculations. Under ModCo and Coinsurance FWH, the ceding company retains the assets on its balance sheet, while the reinsurer receives investment income or other contractual returns. These assets are reported on the ceding company’s Schedule filings and receive an RBC offset, reflecting the risks borne by the reinsurer. The key difference is a ModCo transaction has the reinsurer recognize the reserve liability, while the ceding company recognizes a reserve credit. In contrast, under Coinsurance FWH, the ceding company retains both the assets and the reserves, with the reinsurer recognizing a receivable. This is all fine so long as the accounting and RBC treatment is consistent and transparent - more on this in a bit.

Ceding ModCo and FWH transactions are significant, totaling $925 billion in ModCo reserve credit and $473 billion in FWH at the end of 2023, and growth aligned with broad growth trends in reinsurance.

[center] [img width=500]https://mcusercontent.com/79c3cb5ae113df9ea96a5e443/images/4176f59e-4f3d-3be1-7d59-adacc784ced7.png[/img][/center]
[center][/center]
[center][img width=500]https://mcusercontent.com/79c3cb5ae113df9ea96a5e443/images/cef04da8-f598-23c7-deed-792bda04d72a.png[/img][/center]

[b]What's changing, why is it changing, and why should the investment community care?[/b] The trends are significant. While reinsurance trends are not a concern in themselves, regulators are looking for transparency and to introduce clear guidelines to ensure the industry maintains consistent reporting, which is critical. Regulators are proposing refinements in several dimensions:
[ml][ul][li indent=0 align=left][b]Reserving[/b]. The primary focus is addressing potential concerns with life insurers entering into reinsurance transactions that materially lower total asset requirement (TAR), which is the sum of reserves and required capital. In particular, in non-U.S. transactions, where the reinsurer can take more aggressive investment strategies, possibly limiting the assets supporting the transactions, where regulators have limited visibility to the total assets supporting the block of business. While some risk is medicated through ModCo and FWH transactions, expect additional guardrails and reporting. [/li][ul data=1][li indent=1 align=left][b]Implications for the investment community[/b]. Possible additional restrictions on investment strategies, particularly those associated with non-U.S. transactions, including ModCo and FWH transactions. Notably, assets that support these transactions are not currently identified in Schedule filings, and their earmarked use may not be evident, which might change. [/li][/ul][li indent=0 align=left][b]Identifying assets in FWH or ModCo transactions. [/b]The SAPWG is proposing revisions to annual reporting, [url=https://content.naic.org/sites/default/files/inline-files/24-07%20-%20Modco%20Reporting_0.docx]ModCo[/url] (SAPWG#2024-07). NAIC staff's initial recommendation is to add signifiers, similar in structure to Schedule DL, for assets held under FWH and ModCo arrangements. NAIC staff is requesting a reconciliation of submitted comments suggesting insurers cannot identify assets withheld that are not held in trust and the observed requirements to calculate risk-based capital and fair value of those assets, which suggest those assets are identifiable.[/li][ul data=1][li indent=1 align=left][b]Implications for the investment community[/b]. New requirements to identify assets supporting ModCo and FWH transactions will likely be coupled with RBC offset standards, which can have implications for investment strategy depending on the variation in practice across the industry.[/li][/ul][li indent=0 align=left][b]Assets involved in FWH or ModCo transactions (SAPWG#2024-20)[/b] ([url=https://content.naic.org/sites/default/files/national_meeting/Meeting%20Materials%20SAPWG.pdf]Meeting Materials Attachment B[/url]). SAPWG is proposing revisions that would, in effect, limit the use of assets involved in FWH or ModCo transactions. The proposal does not capture ModCo or FWH assets in the restricted asset reporting that flows through to the general interrogatories, which results in additional “noncontrolled” asset RBC. SAPWG is also sending the Life Risk-Based Capital (E) Working Group (LRBCWG) a referral to clarify the treatment of ModCo assets held by a ceding entity that are pledged or used by the ceding entity for other purposes. SAPWG advocates for limitations to those assets receiving an RBC credit when used as collateral in FHLB transactions, or repurchase or securities lending agreements.[/li][ul data=1][li indent=1 align=left][b]Implications for the investment community[/b]. The proposal would effectively restrict the use of assets supporting ModCo or FWH transactions for other purposes.[/li][/ul][/ul][/ml]

Structural Shifts in the Life Insurance Sector - Consultation
resources_with_topic.notes:
The International Association of Insurance Supervisors (IAIS) published its March 2025 draft Issue Paper on [color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://www.iais.org/uploads/2025/03/Public-consultation-Draft-Issues-Paper-on-structural-shifts-in-the-life-insurance-sector.pdf][i]Structural Shifts in the Life Insurance Sector[/i][/url][/color] for public comment. The paper analyzes structural shifts within the life insurance sector, focusing on the increased allocation to alternative assets and the adoption of asset-intensive reinsurance (AIR) agreements. It highlights the benefits of alternative assets offering diversification, higher potential returns, and alignment with long-term liabilities. However, these benefits come with risks like valuation uncertainty, illiquidity, and complexity, requiring robust risk management. The paper includes potential areas for enhancement in the IAIS supervisory material. The Issue Paper explores:
[ml][ul][li indent=0 align=left]Investments in alternative assets.[/li][ul data=1][li indent=1 align=left]A principles-based definition of alternative assets, focusing on valuation uncertainty, illiquidity, and complexity. [/li][li indent=1 align=left]Current trends in alternative assets and their liquidity and credit risk. [/li][li indent=1 align=left]An evaluation of credit ratings and regulatory capital-related issues associated with alternative assets.[/li][/ul][li indent=0 align=left]Jurisdictional differences in reserving, capital requirements, investment flexibility, and the rise in AIR activity.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]A framework for understanding macroprudential and financial stability implications under the IAIS HF.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]Potential areas for enhancement in the IAIS supervisory material.[/li][/ul][/ml]
[b]Context[/b]. The IAIS represents insurance regulators and supervisors of more than 200 jurisdictions, constituting 97% of the world's insurance premiums. The IAIS is the principal international organization of insurance supervisors, creating international standards and guidelines on insurance supervision. The IAIS has no regulatory power or legal authority, but it influences national and regional regulators by publishing supervisory and supporting material and classifying IAIGs. ICS aspires to create a common language for supervisory discussions of group solvency and enhance global convergence among group capital standards.

[b]The details[/b]. 

[b]Investments in alternative assets. [/b]As explained in the paper, the classification and regulatory treatment of alternative assets varies widely across jurisdictions, reflecting distinct capital market characteristics and stages of development, as well as regulatory frameworks and philosophies. With that in mind, based on IAIS data, in combination with the survey's findings, the paper concludes that while alternative assets currently do not pose a global financial stability concern, there might be prudential supervision concerns for certain insurers—which is largely in line with the findings of the GIMAR 2024. It points to limited holdings by U.S. insurers under Schedule BA-[i]Other Long-Term Investments, [/i]although it acknowledges that alternative assets structured as bonds under Schedule BA have characteristics of alternative assets. Meanwhile, the paper points to European insurers’ alternative asset holdings of [b]€[/b]1,397.1 billion as of June 2024, backing traditional saving product liabilities, which increased from 8% in 2017 to about 15% in 2023.

One of the primary challenges in regulating and monitoring alternative assets is the absence of a universally accepted definition. Surveys conducted by the IAIS noted that jurisdictions employ various terms such as [i]other assets[/i], [i]non-traditional investments[/i], or [i]high-risk assets[/i] to describe investments that do not fall under more commonly used categories. This lack of definition leads to significant variations in how these assets are classified and subsequently regulated. For instance, the surveys indicated that equity funds and real estate are considered non-traditional assets in some jurisdictions, while in others, they are deemed traditional investments. 

The paper defines alternative assets as follows: 
[center][i]Alternative assets are assets which display a high degree of either valuation uncertainty, illiquidity or complexity, or a combination of these[/i].[/center]
Figure 2, which we reproduce, presents asset classes based on three supervisory rankings using a qualitative assessment.
[center][img width=500]https://mcusercontent.com/79c3cb5ae113df9ea96a5e443/images/896fec54-c5f9-6d15-1eb9-0fce34df5fec.png[/img][/center]
Figure 3, reproduced, equally weights the three supervisory rankings at a globally aggregated level. PE funds meet most of the illiquidity, complexity, and difficulty-to-value principles, followed by hedge funds, infrastructure, private credit, and unlisted property funds.
[center][img width=500]https://mcusercontent.com/79c3cb5ae113df9ea96a5e443/images/245515a4-8ad9-2b90-adf9-7b560316dfe6.png[/img][/center]
[b]The use of agency ratings and regulatory oversight.[/b] The paper highlights the significant variation in practices regarding credit ratings and regulatory capital-related issues associated with alternative assets. Credit ratings play a crucial role in evaluating the risk of alternative assets, particularly those that are complex and less transparent, and are often used by insurers to assess asset quality and determine capital requirements. In the U.S., for example, insurers use external credit ratings to determine capital charges, while other jurisdictions generally rely on internal assessments. The concern, in part, the conflicts of interest, is the potential, for example, to [i]rating shop[/i], where an insurer seeks the most favorable ratings across different agencies. This practice can lead to inflated ratings that do not accurately reflect underlying risk, potentially undermining financial stability. The paper also highlights the different capital requirements across jurisdictions, noting that the rules are changing. For example, the NAIC and other regulators are reviewing capital charges for structured securities, including CLOs, to ensure capital frameworks appropriately reflect underlying risks.

[b]Jurisdictional differences in reserving, capital requirements, and investment flexibility and the rise in AIR activity[/b]. AIR is a reinsurance risk-transfer arrangement between two entities and is characterized by a transfer of significant investment risks associated with some insurance liabilities. The paper goes through an impressive breakdown of the regulatory differences across regimes and the potential incentives each generates, relating them with the material drivers contributing to AIR's growth. It also discusses specific concerns, including recapture risk, counterparty risks, and regulatory arbitrage. Drivers of growth in using AIR are jurisdictional differences in reserving approaches, capital requirements, and investment flexibility. It is also clear that there are other important drivers:
[ml][ul][li indent=0 align=left]For third-party AIR, there may be an ability to indirectly benefit from greater investment flexibility or lower cost of capital of the reinsurer or their affiliated alternative asset manager in the form of improved reinsurance pricing.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]For third-party AIR, ceding insurers also noted the increased appetite for legacy blocks of business from AIR reinsurers. AIR allows ceding insurers to exit some markets or blocks of business effectively to refocus their attention on managing their priority products.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]For affiliated AIR, there may be a preference for risks to be transferred to a market-consistent regime, as this allows for better risk measurement and hedging and reduces what some view as redundant capital and reserve requirements.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]For affiliated AIR, access to capital and financing markets may also be a driver, as one jurisdiction may have greater access to global investors than another, even if it may come at the cost of greater total asset requirements (TAR).[/li][/ul][/ml][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]Thematically, the most significant differences are attributed to the degree to which supervisors prescribe assumptions compared to jurisdictions that allow more insurer discretion in setting assumptions, subject to supervisor oversight and limitations.[/color]


[b][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]A framework for understanding macroprudential and financial stability implications under the IAIS HF[/color][/b][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]. The key objective of macroprudential policy for the insurance sector is to ensure that the financial system and insurers can absorb rather than amplify adverse shocks. While alternative asset investments and AIR offer several benefits, they can pose financial stability risks. The [/color][color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://www.iais.org/uploads/2022/01/191114-Holistic-Framework-for-Systemic-Risk.pdf][i]IAIS Holistic Framework for Systemic Risk in Insurance (HF)[/i][/url][/color][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)] recognizes three key transmission channels for systemic risk: asset liquidation, interconnectedness (direct and indirect exposure channel), and critical functions. The insurance sector is linked to several key participants in the financial system and the real economy:[/color]
[ml][ul][li indent=0 align=left]Alternative assets can be funding vehicles for real economy borrowers such as infrastructure projects, real estate, corporates, and consumers (as a key driver under securitization);[/li][li indent=0 align=left]Life insurers have long-dated commitments to policyholders in the real economy. This could involve pensioners who depend on the continued financial condition of the insurance sector for their pensions, which is a direct part of aggregate demand in the real economy;[/li][li indent=0 align=left]Life reinsurers in AIR arrangements may provide commitments to several insurers from across the globe;[/li][li indent=0 align=left]Life insurers are also increasingly connected to a broader range of financial market participants, like banks and alternative asset managers, either as providers of finance or through the distribution of risks. Consequently, disruptions in the life insurance sector can be transmitted to the financial system and real economy.[/li][/ul][/ml][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]The paper identifies three key risks:[/color]
[ml][ol][li indent=0 align=left]Insurers’ forced liquidation of assets in stress;[/li][li indent=0 align=left]Rapid pullback of insurers from key lending markets due to defaults and downgrades;[/li][li indent=0 align=left]Financial market disruption from a mass recapture of AIR by one or many insurers.[/li][/ol][/ml]
[b][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]Potential areas for enhancement in the IAIS supervisory material[/color][/b][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]. The analysis identified gaps in supervisory approaches across different Insurance Core Principles (ICPs), highlighting areas that require updates to ensure effective risk management, including:[/color]
[ml][ul][li indent=0 align=left]ICP 3: Information Sharing and Confidentiality Requirement. Strengthen collaboration between insurance and non-insurance supervisors, particularly for monitoring cross-border AIR agreements.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]ICP 7: Corporate Governance. Require strong governance frameworks for insurers involved in complex reinsurance agreements or alternative investments​.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]ICP 8: Risk Management and Internal Control. Ensure insurers have the technical expertise to assess alternative asset risks, reinsurance complexities, and embedded leverage.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]ICP 9: Supervisory Review and Reporting. Expand supervisory review to assess liquidity risks, recapture risk, and exposures to alternative assets.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]ICP 13: Reinsurance and Other Forms of Risk Transfer. Improve transparency on affiliated reinsurance transactions and their impact on capital adequacy and liquidity.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]ICP 14: Valuation. Ensure valuation methodologies appropriately capture illiquidity, complexity, and hidden leverage in alternative assets.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]ICP 15: Investments. Improved risk management over investment concentrations that take into consideration direct risks (e.g., recapture risk) and indirect risks (e.g., underlying concentration to credit risks in the AIR collateral pools).[/li][li indent=0 align=left]ICP 16: Enterprise Risk Management (ERM). Strengthen risk identification processes for liquidity risks, hidden leverage, and asset-liability mismatches in AIR agreements.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]ICP 20: Public Disclosure. Improve disclosure requirements on alternative asset investments, valuation methodologies, and stress testing results.[/li][/ul][/ml][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)] [/color]
[b]What's next?[/b] The public comment period ends on May 19, 2025, and a background session webinar will be held on April 3, 2025, 7:00 – 8:30 AM ET (register [color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://www.iais.org/events/public-background-session-for-the-draft-issues-paper-on-structural-shifts-in-the-life-insurance-sector/]here[/url][/color]). [i]Questions for public consultation on draft Issues Paper on structural shifts in the life insurance[/i] sector can be accessed ([color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://www.iais.org/uploads/2025/03/Questions-for-public-consultation-on-draft-Issues-Paper-on-structural-shifts-in-the-life-insurance-sector.docx]here[/url][/color]). The final paper will incorporate consultation feedback and be published later in 2025.

[b]As a reminder, while the IAIS has no legal authority, its standards, guidelines, and recommendations influence national and regional regulators, shaping supervisory frameworks and insurance regulatory policies worldwide.[/b]

resources_with_topic.Name: Middle Market (MM) Collateralized Loan Obligations (CLOs) Primer
resources_with_topic.notes:
Middle Market CLOs

[b]The NAIC Capital Markets Bureau published its report, [/b][color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://content.naic.org/sites/default/files/capital-markets-mm-clo-primer.pdf][b][i]Middle Market (MM) Collateralized Loan Obligations (CLOs) Primer[/i][/b][/url][/color], which provides an overview of the MM CLO market.[b] [/b]A few noteworthy learning points:
[ml][ul][li indent=0 align=left]MM CLOs are a subsector of the broader CLO market, where the underlying collateral is predominantly senior secured loans to small and medium-sized companies or those with less than $100 million in earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]Traditionally, MM CLOs are structured as balance sheet transactions, where loans directly made to companies by the asset managers are securitized and moved off the balance sheet as a financing trade to free up the ability to generate new loans. Asset managers and their affiliates often retain the equity tranche of the MM CLOs they originate, aligning their interests with those of the transaction’s remaining investors. Unlike broadly syndicated loans (BSL) CLOs, the capital structure of MM CLOs may be tailored to meet the needs of a specific investor group and have more diverse structures than BSL CLOs.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]MM loans have no secondary market, limited data, and pricing availability compared to BSLs, which have a secondary market; they also tend to have tighter covenants than BSLs.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]Because of the credit risk of the underlying collateral, MM CLOs are structured with additional credit enhancement to protect the investors' interests. They offer a significant spread for comparable credit risk. MM CLOs are becoming a significant proportion of the overall CLO market, especially as the private credit market has expanded.[/li][/ul][/ml][center][img width=500]https://mcusercontent.com/79c3cb5ae113df9ea96a5e443/images/9db3f4b8-ed8f-31ab-c5af-981884fef7e2.png[/img][/center]

resources_with_topic.notes:
Life Insurers’ Role in Public and Private Credit to Risky Firms

[b]Researchers at the Federal Reserve published their report, [/b][color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://www.federalreserve.gov/econres/notes/feds-notes/life-insurers-role-in-the-intermediation-chain-of-public-and-private-credit-to-risky-firms-20250321.html][i]Life Insurers’ Role in the Intermediation Chain of Public and Private Credit to Risky Firms[/i][/url][/color], which explores:
[ml][ul][li indent=0 align=left]Life directly and indirectly (e.g., through investments in CLO tranches and business development corporations (BDCs)) investment trends in sub-investment grade debt.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]The increasing trend of asset managers affiliated with life insurers originating sub-investment grade debt.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]The motivating factors for shifting investment strategies.[/li][/ul][/ml]
[color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]The familiar investment trends across the industry are shown to be more pronounced for life companies with investment management affiliates with a footprint in the space, as seen in Figure 4, reproduced: [/color]
 
[center][img width=500]https://mcusercontent.com/79c3cb5ae113df9ea96a5e443/images/a5b5d06e-dae3-56f5-e1cd-9e751adbf2bb.png[/img][/center]
The report points to the U.S. and Bermudian regulatory frameworks as motivating factors for shifting investment strategies and the ability to use certain vehicles (e.g., BDC) to further leverage investments.

resources_with_topic.Name: Functioning of the E.U. Securitization Regulations
resources_with_topic.notes:
E.U. Regulators Assess Securitization Regulations

The Joint Committee (JC) of the European Supervisory Authorities (ESAs) published its March 31, 2025, report evaluating the [color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://www.eiopa.europa.eu/document/download/841030fd-55a0-4d46-801c-44b540a0876f_en?filename=Joint%20Committee%20report%20on%20the%20functioning%20of%20the%20securitisation%20regulation%20-%20March%202025.pdf][i]Functioning of the E.U. Securitisation Regulations[/i][/url][/color]. The report has two main objectives:
[ml][ol][li indent=0 align=left]Assess the extent to which the securitization regulatory framework (i.e., Regulation (E.U.) 2017/2402, SECR) has achieved its original policy objectives since its implementation in 2019.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]Provide legislative recommendations to the European Commission, including simplifying and strengthening the E.U. securitization framework and safeguarding financial stability.[/li][/ol][/ml]
[b][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]Context[/color][/b][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]. The European Supervisory Authorities (ESAs), the trifectorate of E.U. rulemaking bodies, are comprised of the European Banking Authority (EBA), the European Insurance and Occupational Pension Authority (EIOPA), and European Securities and Markets Authority (ESMA). They are responsible for preparing technical standards for the European Commission based on E.U. Regulations and Directives.[/color]

[color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]Acknowledging that securitization can provide greater financing opportunities, on October 9, 2024, the European Commission requested comment on the [/color][color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://finance.ec.europa.eu/document/download/fb451cdc-4e5b-4d74-9411-cb8bd0789090_en?filename=2024-eu-securitisation-framework-consultation-document_en.pdf][i]Functioning of the Securitization Framework[/i][/url][/color][i][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]. [/color][/i][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]Subsequently, it provided the [/color][color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://finance.ec.europa.eu/document/download/35846dc8-8ebc-4bc9-967d-de77ef5d9234_en?filename=2024-eu-securitisation-framework-summary-of-responses_en.pdf][i]Summary Report of the Targeted Consultation on the Functioning of the E.U. Securitization Framework[/i][/url][/color][i][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]. [/color][/i][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]The JC report focuses on recommendations to enhance the functioning of the SECR, with recommendations that are aligned with those of the Financial Stability Board’s (FSB) report on the [/color][color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://www.fsb.org/uploads/P220125-1.pdf][i]Effects of the G20 Financial Regulatory Reforms on Securitisation[/i][/url][/color][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)] (see [/color][color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://mailchi.mp/bridgewayanalytics/newsreel-17316436?e=9ea3ec665c]ART Newsreel | January 23, 2024[/url][/color][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]). The conclusions of the FSB report are contentious, with many [/color][color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://www.fsb.org/uploads/P220125-2.pdf]respondents arguing[/url][/color][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)] that the reforms impeded E.U. securitization market development compared to the U.S., which did not implement comparable reforms. Stakeholder responses to the ESA report repeatedly point to overly conservative capital charges, regulatory complexity, and a lack of investor confidence—especially outside STS deals.[/color]

[b][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]What's new? [/color][/b][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]The report identifies areas where the regulatory and supervisory framework can be enhanced, supporting the growth of robust and sound securitization markets in Europe:[/color]
[b][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]1. Clarifying the jurisdictional scope.[/color][/b][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)] Clarify that SECR should only apply if at least one securitization party (sell-side or buy-side) is established within the E.U.[/color]
[b][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]2. Broadening the definition of public securitization. [/color][/b][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]The recommended scope would include issues (1) with a prospectus approved under the E.U. Prospectus Regulation, (2) admitted to trading on E.U.-regulated markets or multilateral trading facilities (MTFs), or[/color]
[color=rgb(34, 34, 34)](3) marketed broadly with non-negotiable terms and subject to a market test requiring E.U. originators or sponsors to demonstrate that transactions are not offered to an undefined public.[/color]
[b][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]3. Introducing proportionality in due diligence requirements.[/color][/b][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)] The report calls for more proportionate and practical due diligence requirements, enabling institutional investors to receive data in formats that support meaningful risk assessment. It also calls for commitments from sell-side parties to provide ongoing information throughout the transaction's life.[/color]
[b][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]4. Targeted changes to the STS framework. [/color][/b][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]Under Solvency II, the treatment of securitizations is primarily determined by two characteristics: (1) whether a tranche is the senior-most and (2) whether the structure is classified as simple, transparent, and standardized (STS). Tranches lacking either of these characteristics are subject to significantly higher capital charges. Actively managed structures like Collateralised Loan Obligations (CLOs) or Commercial Mortgage-Backed Securities (CMBS) with mismatched maturity profiles typically fail to meet STS criteria and are thus penalized. The report proposes focused adjustments to improve the efficiency of the STS framework, particularly on-balance-sheet securitizations introduced under the Capital Markets Recovery Package, which aims to support real-economy lending. For context, the European banks rely much more heavily on on-balance-sheet securitizations.[/color]
[b][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]5. Clarifying risk retention rules.[/color][/b][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)] Clearer guidance on risk retention is recommended to reduce interpretation challenges, particularly for Collateralised Loan Obligations (CLOs) and including the term [/color][i][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]predominant source of revenues[/color][/i][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)].[/color]
[b][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]6. Simplifying transparency and reporting requirements.[/color][/b][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)] Recommendations include streamlining reporting templates for public securitizations, improving data standardization, and introducing flexibility to use aggregated or stratified data for certain asset classes. The report also suggests targeted exemptions for small and medium-sized reporting entities to reduce compliance burdens.[/color]
[b][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]7. Promoting greater supervisory consistency across the E.U. [/color][/b][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]The need for more substantial supervisory convergence is highlighted to prevent fragmentation and ensure consistent application across the E.U.[/color]

[b][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]The details.[/color][/b]The report highlights the growth in the E.U. securitization market, especially following the introduction of the STS framework for synthetic securitizations in 2021. By 2022, the share of synthetic securitizations reached 53%, up from 32% in 2020, underscoring the increasing use of synthetic securitizations as a tool for capital management and risk transfer (Figure 1 reproduced). Of course, the market is a tiny fraction of the U.S. market (see ESMA report on [color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://www.esma.europa.eu/sites/default/files/2023-09/ESMA50-524821-2908_TRV_risk_analysis_-_EU_securitisation_markets_overview.pdf]E.U. Securitization Market[/url][/color]) and is still underperforming relative to its pre-crisis peak.
[center][img width=500]https://mcusercontent.com/79c3cb5ae113df9ea96a5e443/images/35cb1a57-5084-9401-2a90-2de4b3d9f90a.png[/img][/center]

The report points to the growth in STS transactions, which comprised 21% of transactions in 2020 and reached 55% of the total E.U. securitization issuance volumes in 2023 (Figure 4 reproduced).
 
[center][img width=500]https://mcusercontent.com/79c3cb5ae113df9ea96a5e443/images/8d5c7a27-0959-3b86-bb75-9a53972245bd.png[/img][/center]

[b]What's next?[/b] The European Commission is expected to use the Joint Committee’s findings and the results of the targeted consultation to inform potential legislative amendments to SECR. These may be proposed later in 2025 as part of the broader Capital Markets Union (CMU) initiative. A synopsis report will accompany the Commission’s impact assessment, incorporating market feedback, with further consultations and feasibility studies likely to follow before any formal legislative proposals are introduced.

resources_with_topic.Name: Matching Adjustment Investment Accelerator (MAIA) (CP7/25)
resources_with_topic.notes:
The U.K. Prudential Regulation Authority (PRA) is proposing to introduce the [color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://www.bankofengland.co.uk/prudential-regulation/publication/2025/april/matching-adjustment-investment-accelerator-consultation-paper]Matching Adjustment Investment Accelerator (MAIA)[/url][/color] (CP7/25), which will remove the requirement to obtain prior approval before a firm can claim a Matching Adjustment (MA) benefit on certain assets. The intent is to allow insurers to take advantage of investment opportunities quickly and promote growth and competitiveness in the U.K. insurance sector. The MA allows insurers to adjust the discount rate used to value long-term insurance liabilities when those liabilities are closely matched with eligible assets, reducing balance sheet volatility. By providing capital relief, the MA incentivizes insurers to invest in long-duration, illiquid assets such as infrastructure loans and private debt. This supports more stable returns and enhances insurers’ ability to make long-term, productive investments aligned with their liability profiles. The effort aligns with the PRA's 2025 thematic priorities, last discussed in [color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://mailchi.mp/bridgewayanalytics/newsreel-17316102?e=9ea3ec665c]ART Newsreel | January 16, 2025[/url][/color].

[b]Context.[/b] After leaving the E.U., the various U.K. government organs have been working on updating legislation to remove deference to E.U. rules. This means a customized version of Solvency II for the U.K. insurance industry, focusing on two key streams: (i) competitiveness and (ii) investment flexibility. Most of the details are now formal policies and are being implemented. The PRA had set up a new MA permissions team, which allows for assessing MA applications more quickly and facilitates the broader range of investment opportunities available within the MA portfolio. The new MA guidelines allowed U.K. firms more flexibility by expanding the set of assets given preferential treatment through ALM initiatives.
[b]The proposed MAIA[/b]. The MAIA framework removes the need for prior PRA approval before including certain types of assets in MA portfolios—provided specific conditions are met.
[ml][ul][li indent=0 align=left]Self-assessed eligibility. Firms with existing MA permission can apply for MAIA permission, allowing for a limited quantity of new asset types (those not previously covered under their MA permission) without waiting for PRA approval.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]Immediate capital benefit. MA benefits can be claimed immediately upon adding MAIA assets.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]Governance and controls[/li][ul data=1][li indent=1 align=left]Maintain a MAIA policy.[/li][li indent=1 align=left]Establish contingency plans in case assets are later deemed ineligible.[/li][li indent=1 align=left]MAIA use report.[/li][li indent=1 align=left]Two-year grace period to submit a formal application allowing for the use of assets in their MA permission.[/li][/ul][li indent=0 align=left]Relevant asset classes. PS10/24 – [i]Reform of the Matching Adjustment[/i], incorporated a framework permitting assets that do not have fixed[/li][li indent=0 align=left]cash flows, expanding the range of insurance businesses that may claim the MA, and removing the restriction on the amount of MA that may be claimed from sub-investment grade assets. Firms granted MAIA permission could include a limited quantity of self-assessed MA-eligible assets with features for which the firm does not already hold MA permission.[/li][/ul][/ml][b]What’s Next? [/b]The consultation (i.e., request for comment) ends June 4, 2025, with implementation in 2025.

resources_with_topic.Name: International Portfolio Frictions
resources_with_topic.notes:
The European Insurance and Occupational Pensions Authority (EIOPA) published its report on [color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://www.eiopa.europa.eu/document/download/5eb5d143-c355-49b2-9427-75ded912dc3c_en?filename=EIOPA%20Occasional%20Research%20Paper%20-%20International%20Portfolios%20Frictions%20-%20April%202025.pdf][i]International Portfolio Frictions[/i][/url][/color][i] [/i]in collaboration with external researchers. The research reveals international portfolio frictions – barriers or challenges European investors face when attempting to diversify their portfolios across borders – that traditional frictions like home country or home currency biases cannot explain. Several patterns may be worth looking into for those of you active with cross-border insurers.  
[ml][ul][li indent=0 align=left]Despite having access to global capital markets and being regulated under common pan-European regulations, insurers' portfolio allocations show large cross-country heterogeneity/ Government bonds comprise a wide range across countries, from less than 20% for insurers domiciled in Norway to ~90% for insurers domiciled in Hungary. The standard deviation across countries of the portfolio share in corporate bonds equals 19% across countries.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]The composition of domestic fixed-income markets, as measured by the share of corporate bonds in outstanding domestic fixed-income instruments, is strongly positively correlated with the fraction of the overall portfolio invested in corporate bonds. This is surprising since European insurers do not exhibit a strong bias toward their home country. In fact, the share of total investments invested domestically averages less than 50%. The relationship also holds within the euro area, implying that home currency bias cannot explain the fact.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]In countries where insurers have higher corporate bond shares in their domestic fixed-income portfolios, their foreign investments tend to resemble their domestic portfolio and feature more corporate bonds. A standard portfolio model predicts that while domestic investments track domestic characteristics due to home bias, foreign investments should offset the characteristics of domestic financial markets.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]While the holdings of foreign bonds resemble the domestic portfolio, one might expect that when a multinational insurer operates a subsidiary in another jurisdiction, they use this as an opportunity to form a globally optimal portfolio. In contrast to this hypothesis, the facts point to the opposite, with local subsidiaries of multinational groups having similar portfolio allocations as other local insurers in their market. The fact that large multinational companies do not use their holding of foreign bonds or use their overseas subsidiaries to form globally optimal portfolios suggests that informational frictions and foreign investment expertise are unlikely to be primary drivers of these biases.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]Asset allocation decisions vary between countries with different domestic government bond yields and sovereign credit risk levels. An insurer operating in a country with high domestic government yields, which tends to be a country with higher sovereign credit risk or inflation risk, tends to hold a portfolio of foreign bonds that earns a higher yield (and is likely to be riskier). This is another example of “domestic projection bias.”[/li][/ul][/ml][color=rgb(34, 34, 34)]The study concludes that the characteristics of domestic financial markets dictate European insurers’ overall portfolio allocation. This allocation pattern occurs despite a significant fraction of their assets being invested abroad and despite active local market participation by large multinational insurance groups.[/color]

resources_with_topic.Name: IAIS Application Paper on the Supervision of Climate-Related Risks in the Insurance Sector
resources_with_topic.notes:
IAIS Climate-Related Recommendations for Supervision of Investment 

The International Association of Insurance Supervisors (IAIS) published its April 16, 2025, [url=https://www.iais.org/uploads/2025/04/Application-Paper-on-the-supervision-of-climate-related-risks-in-the-insurance-sector.pdf?utm_source=International+Association+of+Insurance+Supervisors+%28IAIS%29&utm_campaign=0c841f4467-EMAIL_CAMPAIGN_2025_04_15_05_38&utm_medium=email&utm_term=0_-0c841f4467-634247980][i]Application Paper on the Supervision of Climate-Related Risks in the Insurance Sector[/i][/url], following four consultations. The Application Paper[i] [/i]provides an overview of how the IAIS’ Insurance Core Principles (ICPs) can be applied to address climate-related risks. 

[b]Context[/b]. The IAIS represents insurance regulators and supervisors of more than 200 jurisdictions, constituting 97% of the world's insurance premiums. The IAIS is the principal international organization of insurance supervisors, creating international standards and guidelines on insurance supervision, including Insurance Core Principles (ICPs), which are comprised of Principle Statements, Standards, and Guidance, as a globally accepted framework for insurance supervision. The IAIS has no regulatory power or legal authority.

[b]What's new?[/b] The Application Paper is intended to support supervisors in integrating climate-related risks into their supervision and makes several recommended changes to ICPs, including ICP-15, which provides supervisory guidance over insurers' investments:
[ml][ol][li indent=0 align=left]When establishing regulatory investment requirements on the investment activities of the insurer, the supervisor may consider the potential impact of climate change on the insurer’s investments.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]It is important that insurers invest in assets so that their portfolio as a whole considers the impact of their investments on the climate ([i]outward perspective[/i]). It is also important that insurers consider their policyholders’ preferences about climate change considerations, where relevant.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]Insurers should consider climate-related risks as part of their asset-liability management (ALM), especially for long-duration liabilities.[/li][li indent=0 align=left]Supervisors should assess how insurers consider climate-related risks when identifying, assessing, monitoring, managing, controlling, and reporting risks arising from their investments.[/li][/ol][/ml]While the second recommendation, an [i]outward perspective [/i]of investment strategies, enters into an area that has proven contentious at times, it is important to note that Application Papers provide supporting material related to ICPs but do not include new requirements.

resources_with_topic.Name: Non-Bond Debt
resources_with_topic.notes:
[center]IN THE WEEDS[/center]
[center]With Brett Manning and Nitsa Einan[/center]
[center][/center]
[center]Non-Bond Debt[/center]
[center] [/center]
[center][img width=500]https://mcusercontent.com/79c3cb5ae113df9ea96a5e443/images/bb1e6e72-ee8d-b15e-7244-9352a67b4fee.png[/img][/center]

The upcoming Property & Casualty Risk-Based Capital (E) Working Group (P&C-RBC-WG) meeting agenda (see [color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://us8.campaign-archive.com/?e=__test_email__&u=79c3cb5ae113df9ea96a5e443&id=0000085296#P&C]RBC for Non-Bond Debt Held by P&C and Health[/url][/color]) motivated us to explore investment patterns and treatment of non-bond debt. Assets with debt characteristics that fail to qualify as bonds are not reported on Schedule D. Mortgages, for example, are explicitly excluded from bond qualification and reported on Schedule B, while debt securities that fail bond qualification can fall under [i]SSAP 21-Other Admitted Assets[/i]. Depending on the form of the debt, whether it qualifies as a security, for example, can result in failing bond qualification and landing on Schedule BA, with implications for reporting and treatment - a perfect topic for this week's [i]In the Weeds[/i] - let's dive in!

We explored [i]Non-Bond Debt & Schedule BA Assets [/i][color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://mailchi.mp/bridgewayanalytics/newsreel-17315959?e=9ea3ec665c]ART Newsreel | January 9, 2024[/url][/color], [i]In the Weeds[/i], focusing on broader Schedule BA investment patterns and the new categories of [b]Debt [i]Securities[/i] that fail bond qualification[/b]:
[ml][ul][li indent=0 align=center][b]Does not reflect a creditor relationship in substance[/b]. For example, principal-protected securities (PPS) might combine a treasury security with a performance asset (e.g., a call on the S&P 500) to produce an asset with high total return risk and minimal contractual risk of default. While PPS can receive a favorable agency rating, the performance component will result in failing bond qualification.[/li][li indent=0 align=center][b]Lacks substantive credit enhancement[/b]. As discussed in the Implementation Q&A, CMBS SASBs with insufficient subordination, including uni-tranche SASBs, are not expected to qualify as bonds, regardless of the loan-to-value or over-collateralization of the property compared to the underlying mortgage loan. Notably, the bond definition does not contemplate a broad look-through of the underlying collateral to indirect subordination.[/li][li indent=0 align=center][b]Backed by non-financial assets that lack meaningful cash flows[/b]. An example is a debt security that relies on the underlying collateral retaining its value to repay the debt (e.g., through the sale of collateral or refinancing), such as non-cashflow-producing real estate at a 50% loan-to-value. While it would not qualify to be reported as a bond, its characteristics are consistent with those of a mortgage loan and may warrant a fixed income RBC charge.[/li][/ul][/ml]These debt securities fall under [i]SSAP 21-Other Admitted Assets[/i], and receive the following treatment:
[ml][ul][li indent=0 align=left][b]Valuation. [/b]Reported at the lower of amortized cost or fair value, with changes recorded as unrealized gains or losses. The treatment is similar to how, say, non-life companies value impaired debt on Schedule D.[/li][li indent=0 align=left][b]Designations. [/b]ABS, which fail the bond definition, are eligible for NAIC Designations, with rating-based Designations not allowable. [/li][li indent=0 align=left][b]Capital. [/b]While property & casualty Schedule BA charges are punitive at 20%, life companies Schedule BA C-1 charges are pre-2021 and can be more favorable than Schedule D-1. Schedule BA C-1 charge is based on the coarse pre-2021 C-1 factors, which can be seen in the figure below in blue, along with the more granular Schedule D C-1 charge in orange, across Designations (with associated Moody's ratings for context). Since the more granular Schedule D charge straddles the Schedule BA charge, a move to Schedule BA can provide capital relief, which is seemingly counterintuitive. RBC C-1 would decrease by over 60% for debt with a Designation 1G (~Moody's Rating A3) as a result of moving from Schedule D (C-1 of 1.02%) to Schedule BA (C-1 of 0.39%). We encourage regulators to review the capital treatment across Schedules and ensure differences are deliberate.[/li][/ul][/ml] 
[center][img width=500]https://mcusercontent.com/79c3cb5ae113df9ea96a5e443/images/2f95285f-fd42-d9fb-4085-0384a47c5953.png[/img][/center]

Let's explore non-bond debt, which fails to qualify as non-bond debt securities. 
[ml][ul][li indent=0 align=left][b]Collateral loans [/b]are unconditional obligations secured by investments. Loan amounts are not admitted if they are in excess of the admitted collateral. Bank loans, as defined by the NAIC, are carved out and quality as bonds, despite generally not qualifying as securities. The definition of a bank loan requires the obligation to be of an operating entity, directly extended by an insurer entity, or acquired through a participation, syndication, or assignment. This means that a special-purpose vehicle (SPV) loan, such as one issued by an asset-backed security (ABS) issuer, would fail to qualify as a bank loan. That loan would be treated as a collateral loan under Schedule BA.[/li][li indent=0 align=left][b]Noncollateral loans [/b]are the unpaid portion of loans for which collateral to secure the loan has not been obtained. [/li][li indent=0 align=left][b]Surplus debentures and capital notes, [/b]under [i]SSAP No. 41—Surplus Notes[/i], do not qualify as bonds since interest or principal can be canceled in the event of financial stress in a non-resolution scenario without triggering a default. Surplus debentures are a subordinate form of capital issued by insurers with payments that their regulators can halt; they are particularly useful for non-stock insurers. Non-insurance entities issue capital notes. As discussed in [color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://us8.campaign-archive.com/?e=__test_email__&u=79c3cb5ae113df9ea96a5e443&id=0000085296#P&C]RBC for Non-Bond Debt Held by P&C and Health[/url][/color], prior to the bond definition, many companies were likely reporting capital notes under Schedule D-1 as bonds. [/li][/ul][/ml][center] [/center]
Surplus debentures have been a consistent investment class for life companies, with holdings in the order of $20 billion. Meanwhile, investments in collateral loans have grown significantly, 2.5-fold over the last five years, which has motivated heightened disclosure requirements and has regulators consider evaluating their capital treatment (see [color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://mailchi.mp/bridgewayanalytics/newsreel-17318577?e=9ea3ec665c]ART Newsreel | March 27, 2025[/url][/color]). Noncolalteeal Loans are inconsiwquential. As discussed in [color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://us8.campaign-archive.com/?e=__test_email__&u=79c3cb5ae113df9ea96a5e443&id=0000085296#P&C]RBC for Non-Bond Debt Held by P&C and Health[/url][/color], the true significance of capital notes will be known once reporting under the bond definition is available.
 
[center][img width=500]https://mcusercontent.com/79c3cb5ae113df9ea96a5e443/images/b4a702bb-35d6-4d96-fe22-9f104d3fe2d2.png[/img][/center]

Investment patterns for property & casualty are noticeably different, likely driven by differences in how those assets are treated - see below. Noncollateral loans aside, reported holdings are inconsequential. Notably, of the $8 billion in industry noncollateral loans held at year-end 2024, ~$6 billion was held by a single company. The significant drop in 2020 and 2021 was driven by that same company and another company that no longer invests in noncollateral loans.  
 
[center][img width=500]https://mcusercontent.com/79c3cb5ae113df9ea96a5e443/images/5ad99c21-4855-528e-5f00-9f663c07fd81.png[/img][/center]


Collateral loans and noncollateral loans fall under [i]SSAP 21-Other Admitted Assets,[/i] while surplus debentures and capital notes fall under [i]SSAP No. 41—Surplus Notes[/i], and receive roughly the following treatment:
[ml][ul][li indent=0 align=left][b]Valuation. [/b][/li][ul data=1][li indent=1 align=left][b]Collateral and noncollateral loans[/b] are reported at the lower of amortized cost or fair value, with changes recorded as unrealized gains or losses. The treatment is similar to how, say, non-life companies value impaired debt on Schedule D.[/li][li indent=1 align=left][b]Surplus debentures and capital notes[/b] receive treatment similar to Schedule D - amortized cost for those Designated 1 or NAIC 2, and the lesser of amortized cost or fair value for those with no Designation or Designation 3 through 6. There are additional nuances for, say, transactions with subsidiaries, to avoid double-counting surplus. [/li][/ul][li indent=0 align=left][b]Designations. [/b][/li][ul data=1][li indent=1 align=left][b]Collateral and noncollateral loans[/b] are not eligible for Designations.[/li][li indent=1 align=left][b]Surplus debentures and capital notes[/b] are eligible for agency rating-based Designations.[/li][/ul][li indent=0 align=left][b]Capital. [/b][/li][ul data=1][li indent=1 align=left][b]Collateral loans [/b]receive an RBC charge of 6% for life companies (roughly in line with a BB- rated bond) and a 5% BA charge for property & casualty companies. State treatment can vary significantly. Wisconsin limits the size of the loan to 90% of the market value of collateral securities and requires the collateral securities to be considered against other state limits. Meanwhile, Texas limits life companies from extending a loan that exceeds 67% of the collateral, including corporate capital stock, bonds, bills of exchange, and other qualifying securities. Some states, like New York, place a limit of 5% on aggregate investments in collateral loans; others do not.[/li][li indent=1 align=left][b]Noncollateral loans[/b] receive an RBC charge of 30% for life companies and a 20% charge when held by property & casualty companies.  [/li][li indent=1 align=left][b]Surplus debentures and capital notes[/b] held by property & casualty are charged a punitive 20%, while life company charges are in line with the pre-2021 charges, described above.[/li][/ul][/ul][/ml]
[b]How significant is non-bond debt on Schedule BA? [/b]It's big enough to matter, and uncertain enough to watch closely. Collateral loans have steadily expanded, and surplus debentures remain a meaningful life company investment class. Meanwhile, shifting capital notes and non-bond debt securities to Schedule BA may reveal interesting investment strategies.
[center][/center]
[center][b]We’ll be keeping our eye on this one. Stay tuned![/b][/center]
[center][/center]
[center][b]Need help with classifying assets? Just [/b][color=rgb(31, 41, 157)][url=https://www.bridgewayanalytics.com/contact][b]ask for help[/b][/url][/color][b]![/b][/center]
